30-40% SiOz, 10-20% NaxO, 60 40% H20,
i.e. ~Na2Si,Os.6H20. These find extensive usc
in industrial and domestic liquid detergents
because they maintain high pH by means of their
buffering ability and can saponify animal and
‘vegetable oils and fats: they also emulsify mineral
oils, deflocculate dirt particles, and prevent
redeposition of suspended dirt and soil, The more
ditute solutions (arca 10) are used in production
of silica gels by acidification (pp. 345 and
below). There are numerous other uses of soluble
silicates including adhesives, glues and binders,
especially for corrugated cardboard boxes, and
as refractory acid-resistant cements and sealants.
World production in 1981 was ~3.0 million
tonnes of which the sodium silicates formed the
major part. Price range from $220-450nenne
depending on composition,

 

 

Potassium silicate solutions are equally com:
plex: for example, an aqueous solution prepared
from KOH and SiO} in which the ratio K:Si is
1:1 contains 22 different disercte silicate anions
4s identified by ®°Si COSY mr studies.

The system SiO;-1,0, even in the ahsence
of metal oxides. is particularly complex
and of immense geochemical and industrial
importance.” The mp of pure SiOz decreases
<Gramatically by as much as 800° on addition of
1-26 HO (at high pressure), presumably as a
result of the siructure-breaking effect of replacing

-O-Si links by “terminal” Si-OH groups. With
increasing concentrations of HzO one obtains
hydrated silica gels and colloidal dispersions
Of silica; there are also numerous hydrates. and
distinct silicic acids in very dilute aqueous
solutions, but these tend to be rather insoluble
and rapidly precipitate with further condensation
‘when aqueous solutions of soluble silicates
are acidified, Structural information is sparse,
particularly for the solid state, but in solution
evidence has been claimed for atleast 5 species
(Cable 9.9) itis unlikely that any ofthese species
exist in the solid state since precipitation is
‘accompanied by further condensation and ross
linking to form “polysilicie acids” of indefinite
and vasiable composition [SiO,(OH),-2,]n (€f
B, Al, Fe, ctc.). However, the crystal structure
of (€-CeHti:)»Sin0s{O2WONMep)s)3} has been

9.3.5 Silicate minerals 4)

The earth's crustal rocks and their break-
down products — the various soils, clays and
sands — are composed almost entirely (~95%)
of silicate minerals and silica. This predomi-
nance of silicates and aluminosilicates is reflected
in the abundance of O, Si and Al, which are
the commonest elements in the crust (p. 329).
Despite the great profusion of structural types
and the widely varying stoichiometries which are
unmatched elsewhere in chemistry, it is possible
to classify these structures on the basis of a few
simple principles. Almost invariably Si is coordi-
nated tetrahedrally by 4 oxygen atoms and these
{SiO} units can exist either as discrete structural
entities or can combine by comer sharing of O
atoms into larger units. The resulting O lattice
is frequently close-packed, or approximately so,
and charge balance is achieved by the presence of
further cations in tetrahedral, octahedral, or other
sites depending on their size. Typical examples

are as follows (radii in pm):*

 

 

CN 4 = Lil (59) Bel (27) Atl (39) sil¥ (26)

CN 6 : Nat (102) Mgt (72) Ail (54) ‘THY (61) Fel 78)
Ns: KE CS1) Call (112)

CN 12: Kt (164)

minerals aze the more electropositive metas, Le. those in

with ¥, La and the lanthanoids, Zr, Hf, Th, U and to a much
lesser éxtent the posttransition elements Sal, Pbl, and Bill.

Silicate minerals 47

‘The quoted radii, which in tum depend
on the CN, are the empirical “effective
ionic radii” deduced by R.D. Shannon (and
C.T. Prewitt)*9) and do not imply full charge
separation such as {Si**(O?-),}, etc. Note that
Al™ can occupy either 4- or 6-coordinate sites
so that it can replace either Si or M in
the lattice — this is particularly important in
discussing the structures of the aluminosilicates.
Several other cations can occupy sites of differing
CN, e.g. Li (4 and 6), Na (6 and 8), K (6-12),
though they are most commonly observed in the
CN shown.

As with the borates (p. 205) and to a lesser
extent the phosphates (p. 526), the {SiO4} units
can build up into chains, multiple chains (or
ribbons), rings, sheets and three-dimensional
networks as summarized below and elaborated
in the following paragraphs.

Silicates with discrete units

Discrete (SiO,} units occur in the orthosilicates
MYSiO, (M = Be, Mg, Mn, Fe and Zn) and in
ZSiO, as well as in the synthetic orthosilicates
NaySiO4 and K4SiOs.“®) In phenacite, BerSiO,,
both Be and Si occupy sites of CN 4 and the
structure could equally well be described as a
3D network M3Os. When octahedral sites are
occupied, isomorphous replacement of M" is
often extensive as in olivine, (Mg,Fe.Mn),SiOg
which derives its name from its olive-green
colour (Fe"). In zircon, ZrSiO,, the stoichiometry

‘ation. Another important group of exthosilicates
is the gamets, [MEM!(SiO,)s}. in which Mt
are 8-coordinate (e.g. Ca, Mg, Fe) and M" are
6-coordinate (e.g. Al, C1, Fe). Orthosilicates
are also vital components of Portland cement
{p. 252): B-CasSiO, has discrete {SiOs} groups
with rather irregularly coordinated Ca in sixfold
and eightfold environments (the a-form has
the KsSO, structure and the y-form has the
olivine structure). Again alite, CasSiOs, which
is intimately involved in the “setting” process,
has individual Ca, {SiQ,) and O as the
structural units

importance also emerged, The first was that there
was no significant difference between the Si-O

as would be expected for isolated {Si:0;°}

i. The tings are Inked into
infinite sheets in the (010) planes which are
themselves linked via 2n-O(H) Zn or Si-O-Si
bonds. The 3D framework so generated leaves
large channels which open into large cavities
that accommodate the removable H;0 molecules.
‘The structure is thus very similar in principle to
that of the framework alumsinoslicates (p. 354)
and its conventional description in terms. of
discrete Sig0;° ions is rather misleading and
informative

Alternative representations of the structures are
given in Fig. 9.11, and it is well worth white
ooking carefully at these various diagrams since
they have the pleasing propery of becoming
simpler and easier to understand the longer they
are contemplated, It should be stressed that the
formulae given are ideal limiting compositions
and that Al! or Mg!" can be replaced by
several other cations of appropriate size. The
Stoichiometry is further complicated by the
possibility that Si"¥ can be parly replaced by
‘A in the tetrahedral sites thereby. giv

micas or by layers of thydrated cations to give
montmorillonite. Alternatively, charge balance
can be achieved by interleaving positively
charged (Mg, AIO}: layers as in the chlories.
These possibilities are shown schematically in
Figs. 9.12 and 9.13 (p. 355) and elaborated in the
following paragraphs.

‘When these minerals are heated they dehydrate
in a remarkable way by extruding little worm-
like structures as indicated by their name (Latin
vermiculus, little worm); the resulting porous
light-weight mass is much used for packing and
insulation, The relationship between the various
layer silicates is summarized with idealized
formulae in Table 9.10 (on page 357).

Silicates with framework structures

‘The structural complexity of the 3D framework
aluminosilicates precludes a detailed treatment
here, but many of the minerals are of paramount
importance. The group includes the feldspars
(which are the most abundant of all minerals, and
comprise ~60% of the earth's crust), the zeolites
(which find major applications as molecular
sieves, desiccants, ion exchangers and water
softeners), and the ultramarines which, as their
name implies, often have an intense blue colour,
All are constructed from SiO, units in which each
O atom is shared by 2 tetrahedra (as in the various
forms of SiO» itself), but up to one-half of the Si

Pressure is another important variable in the
formation of feldspars and at sufficiently high
pressures there is a tendency for Al to increase its
coordination number from 4 10 6 with consequent
destruction of the feldspar lattice." For example:

‘grossular

Such reactions marking the disappearance of
plagioclase feldspars may be responsible for the
Mohorovicic discontinuity between the earth’s
crust and mantle: this implies that the crust and
mantle are isocompositional, the crustal rocks
above having phases characteristic of gabbro rock
(olivine, pyroxene, plagioclase) whilst the mantle
rocks below are an eclogite-containing garnet,
Al-rich pyroxene and quartz. Not all geochemists
agree, however.

Zeolites have much more open aluminosili-
cate frameworks than feldspars and this enables
them to take up loosely bound water or other
small molecules in their structure. Indeed, the
name zeolite was coined by the mineralogist

AW(OH), and spinels such as MgAlpOs. Much higher pres-
sures still are required to transform 4-coordinated Si to 6-
‘coordinated (p. 343).

 

@

Ch.9

A. F. Cronstedt in 1756 (etv zein, to boil; 400g
lithos, stone) because the mineral appeared to
boil when heated in the blow-pipe flame. Zeo-
lite structures are characterized by the presence
of tunnels or systems of interconnected cavities;
these can be linked either in one direction giv-
ing fibrous crystals, or more usually in two or
three directions to give lamellar and 3D struc-
tures respectively. Figure 9.15a shows the con-
struction of a single cavity from 24 linked (SiO)
tetrahedra and Fig. 9.15 shows how this can be
conventionally represented by a truncated cubo-
octahedron formed by joining the Si atom posi-
tions. Several other types of polyhedron have also
been observed. These are then linked in three
dimensions to build the aluminosilicate frame-
work. A typical structure is shown in Fig. 9.15¢
for the synthetic zeolite “Linde A” which
has the formula [Nay2(Al;2Si:2O«s)].27H20.°
Other cavity frameworks are found in other
zeolites such as faujasite, which has the ideal-
ized formula [NaCag s(Al,SisO,4)]-10H20, and
chabazite [Ca(Al)Si,)O12].6H,O. There is great
current interest in this field since it offers scope
for the reproducible synthesis of structures hav-
ing cavities, tunnels and pores of precisely
defined dimensions on the atomic scale.“ By

§9.3.6

appropriate design such molecular sieves can be
used to selectively remove water or other small
molecules, to separate normal from branched-
chain paraffins, to generate highly dispersed
metal catalysts, and to promote specific size-
dependent chemical reactions.) Zeolites are
made commercially by crystallizing aqueous gels,
of mixed alkaline silicates and aluminates at
60-100". Zeolite-A is being increasingly used as
a detergent builder to replace sodium tripolyphos-
phate (p. 528).

‘The final group of framework aluminosilicates
are the ultramarines which have alternate Si
and Al atoms at the comers of the polyhedra
shown in Fig.9.15a and b and, in addition,
contain substantial concentrations of anions
such as Cl“, SO, or S)?~. These minerals
tend to be anhydrous, like the feldspars, and
in contrast to the even more open zeolites.
Examples are sodalite (NagCly(AlgSigO2a)],
noselite [Nas(SOs)(AlsSigO>4)] and ultramarine
[Nag(S2)(AlgSi¢Or4)]- Sodalite is colourless if
the supernumerary anions are all chloride, but
partial replacement by sulfide gives the brilliant
blue mineral lapiz lazuli. Further replacement
gives ultramarine which is now manufactured
synthetically as an important blue pigment for
oil-based paints and porcelain, and as a “blueing”
agent to mask yellow tints in domestic washing,

9.3.6 Other inorganic compounds
of silicon

This section briefly considers compounds in
which Si is bonded to elements other than
hydrogen, the halogens or oxygen, especially
compounds in which Si is bonded to S, N
or P. Silicon burns in S vapour at 100° to
give SiS, which can be sublimed in a stream
of No to give long, white, flexible, asbestos-
like fibres, mp 1090°, sublimation 1250°C. The
structure consists of infinite chains of edge-
shared tetrahedra (like W-silica, p. 343) and these
transform at high temperature and pressure to
a (comer-shared) cristobalite modification. The
structural complexity of SiO> is not repeated,
however. SiS» hydrolyses rapidly to SiO» and
H,S and is completely ammonolysed by liquid
NH; to the imide

 

SiS) + 4NH3 —> Si(NH), + 2NH,SH

Sulfides of Na, Mg, Al and Fe convert SiS»
into. metal thiosilicates, and ethanol yields

“ethylsilicate” Si(OEt); and HS. Volatile

thiohalides have been reported from the

‘The elusive SiS grouping has been syn-
thesized by reaction between solid Si and
HS at 1200°C to give monomeric SiS; this
high-temperature molecule can itself be reacted
with Cl or HCI in an argon matrix to yield
monomeric S=SiCl, and S=SiHCI.°* Synthe-
sis of stable organosilanethiones, RR/Si=S has
been achieved by using the strategem of impart-
ing additional stabilization through intramolecu-
lar coordination via an amine function; e.g. ((e-
naphthyl)(8-Me2NCH2CioHs)Si=S] was _pre-
pared by heating the corresponding silane
RR'SiFh with Ss; the SiS distance of 201.3 pm
was noticeably shorter than the normal single
bond Si~S distance of 216 pm.

‘The most important nitride of Si is SisNs;
this is formed by direct reaction of the elements
above 1300° or more economically by heating
SiO» and coke in a stream of N2/H> at 1500°.
‘The compound is of considerable interest as an
engineering material since it is almost completely
inert chemically, and retains its strength, shape
and resistance to corrosion and wear even above
100°. Its great hardness (Mohs 9), high

   

 

‘metal-free silica for use in phosphors in fluorescent lamps,
and TV tubes. In partly hydrolysed form it is used as a
paint vehicle, a protective coating for porous stone, and as
f vehicle for zine-containing galvanic corrosion-preventing.
coatings. Many other orthoesters Si(OR), are known but none
are commercially important

dissociation temperature (1900°, 1 atm) and high
density (3.185gcm~*) can all be related to
its compact structure which resembles that of
phenacite (Be»SiOs, p. 347). It is an insulator
with a resistivity at room temperature ~ 6.6 x
10°ohmem. Another refractory, SizN,O, is
formed when Si+ SiO2 are heated to 1450° in
a stream of Ar containing 5% No. The structure
comprises puckered hexagonal nets of alternating
Si and N atoms interlinked by nonlinear Si-O-Si
bonds to similar nets on either side; the Si atoms
are thus each 4-coordinate and the N atoms
3-coordinate.

Volatile silylamides are readily prepared by
reacting a silyl halide with NH3, RNH> or RNH
in the vapour phase or in E120, e.g.:

3SiH3Cl + 4NHy —> N(SiH); + 3NH,Cl
SiH;Br + 2Me:NH —> SiHsNMe7 + Me2NH2Br
ASiHgI + SN2Hy —> (SiHs)2NN(SiHy)2 + 4NoHsI

Silicon-substituted derivatives may require the
use of lithio or sodio reagents, e.g.

Me,SiCl + NaN(SiMe;)2 —+ N(SiMe;) + NaCl
PhySiLi + RaNH —> PhsSiNR» + LiH

‘The N atom is always tertiary in these compounds
and no species containing the SiH-NH group
is stable at room temperature. Apart from this
restriction, innumerable such compounds have
been prepared including cyclic and polymeric
analogues, e.g. [cyclo-(Me>SiN(SiMe3)>}] and
(cyclo-(MeSiNH),]. Interest has focused on the
stereochemistry of the N atom which is often
planar, or nearly so.°)) Thus N(SiH3)s features a
planar N atom and this has been ascribed to p,-
d, interaction between the “nonbonding” pair of
electrons on N and the “vacant” dy orbitals on Si
as shown schematically in Fig. 9.16. Consistent
with this trisilylamines are notably weaker
ligands than their tertiary amine analogues though,
replacement of one or two SiHls by CH enhances
the donor power again; e.g. N(SiHs)s forms
no adduct with BH; even at low temperature;

Compounds containing an Si=N double
bond are of very recent provenance. The
first stable silanimine, Bu',Si=N-SiBu's, was
prepared in 1986 as pale yellow crystals, mp 85°
(decomp.);@) it features a short Si=N distance
(56.8pm, cf. Si-N 1695 pm) and almost
Finear coordination about the N atom (177.8"),
suggesting some electronic delocalization as
described above. The compound was made
by reacting the aridosilane Bu',SiCK(Ns) with
NaSiBu's in Bu,O at —78°. The related
compound Pr'jSi=NR (R = 2,4,6-Bu'sCoH2~)
forms stable orange crystals, mp 98°.(©

Unusual Si/P compounds are also beginning
to appear, for example, the tetrasilahexaphospha-
adamantane derivative [(Pr'Si)(PH).] (1), which
ismade by reacting Pr'SiCls with Lif AlPH2)4].

9.3.7 Organosilicon compounds
and silicones

Well over 100.000 organosilicon compounds have
been synthesized. Of these, during the past
few decades, silicone oils, elastomers and resins
have become major industrial products. Many
‘organosilicon compounds have considerable
thermal stability and chemical inertness; e.g.
SiPhy can be distilled in air at its bp 428°, as can
PhySiCl (bp 378°) and PhaSiCl (bp 305°). These,
and innumerable similar compounds, reflect the
considerable strength of the SiC bond which is,
indeed, comparable with that of the C-C bond
(p. 338). A further illustration is the compound
SiC which closely resembles diamond in its
properties (p. 334). Catenation and the formation
‘of multiple bonds are further similarities with
carbon chemistry, though these features are
less prominent in organosilicon chemistry and
much of the work in these areas is of recent

©M, DRIESS, A.D. FANTA, D.R.Powstt. and R. West,
‘Anges: Cher. Int, Edn. Engi. 28, VO38~40 (1989).

"N.C. NoRstan, Polyhedron 12, 2431=46 (1983) and
feferences cited therein. -M. DRIESS, Adv. Organomet
Chem. 39, 193-229 (1996) — also deals wih silaarsenes
‘conttining bonds).

# Page 384

362 Silicon Ch.9

origin (e.g. pp. 338 and below). For example,
although the word “silicone” was coined by
F. S. Kipping in 1901 to indicate the similarity
in formula of PhySiO with that of the ketone
benzophenone, Ph;CO, he stressed that there was
no chemical resemblance between them and that
Ph2SiO was polymeric.) It is now recognized
that the great thermal and chemical stability of
the silicones derives from the strength both of
the Si-C bonds and of the Si-O-Si linkages.
Many general reviews of the vast subject of
organosilicon chemistry are available (e.g. refs 1,
2, 69-74) and only some of the salient or
topical features will be touched on here. An
interesting subset comprises the carbosilanes, that
is compounds with a skeleton of alternating C
and Si atoms.) These include chains, rings
and polycyclic compounds, many of which
can be made on a multigram or even larger
scale by controlled thermolysis or by standard
organometallic syntheses.

‘Transient reaction species containing Si=C
bonds have been known since about 1966 and
can be generated thermally, photolytically, or
even chemically. A decade later Me>Si=CHMe

was isolated in low-temperature matrices”®) but,
despite concerted and well-planned attempts over
many years, it was not until 1981 that a stable
silene was reported.” A. G. Brook and his
group prepared 2-adamantyl-2-trimethylsiloxy-
1 L-bis(trimethylsilyl)-I-silaethene as very pale
yellow needles, mp 92°:

. ether/hv
(Me3Si)3SiC(O)(CipHis) =
(Me;Si)2Si=C(OSiMes)(adamantyl)

 

The solid silaethene was stable indefinitely at
room temperature in the absence of air or
other reagents but in solution it slowly reverted
(over several days) to the isomeric acylsilane
starting material. An X-ray analysis confirmed
the structure and revealed a short >Si=C<
bond (176.4pm, cf. 187-191pm for single-
bonded Si-C) and a planar disposition of ipso
atoms, the two planes being slightly twisted
with respect to each other (14.6°). The use of
bulky groups to enhance the stability of the
silaethene is also notable, though this is not
a necessary feature, at least at the Si cen-
tre, since Me2Si=C(SiMes)(SiMeBu') is sta-
ble as colourless crystals at room temperature
(>Si=C< distance 170.2pm, Si-C 189.0pm
and a planar C,Si=CSiz skeleton).®) The not
unrelated planar heterocyclic compounds silaben-
zene, CsSiH,"'9) and silatoluene, CsHsSiMe,!®)
should also be recalled.

Disilenes, containing the grouping >Si=Si<,
can be isolated as thermally stable yellow or
orange crystalline compounds provided that the
substituents are sufficiently large to prevent

polymerization (e.g. mesityl, t-butyl, etc.) The
first such compound, SiyMess, was isolated in
1981 as orange crystals, mp 176°, following
photolysis of the trisilane SiMes;(SiMes)>.
The Si=Si distance in several such com-
pounds falls in the range 214-216pm, which
is about 10% shorter than the normal single-
bonded Si-Si distance. Disilenes are chemically
very reactive. Halogens and HX molecules give
1,2-addition products, e.g. MespSi(Cl)Si(C)-
Mes», whilst aldehydes and ketones undergo
[2+ 2] cyclo-addition reactions to give 1,2,3-
oxadisilenanes, OSi(Mes),Si(Mes)>CHR. Con-
trolled oxidation gives predominantly the 1,2-
dioxetane OSiR,SIR2O (80%), plus the 1,3-
cyclodisiloxane OSIR,OSiR> as a minor product.
‘Numerous other novel heterocyles have been pre-
pared by controlled reactions of disilenes with
chalcogens, N2O, P, and organic nitro-, nitroso-,
azo- and azido-compounds.*") Transition metal
complexes can give 7?-disilene adducts such as
[Pt(PRs)>)(n?-SizMesa)].79)

‘The product, Sis(SiBu's)., forms intensely orange
crystals that are stable to heat, light, water and air,
and do not melt below 350°. The Si~Si distances
within the closo-Si, cluster are 232-234 pm
and the exo Si-Si distances are slightly longer,
235-237 pm (cf. Si-Si 235.17 in crystalline Si).
Comparison with the closo-anion Six*~, which
occurs in several metal silicides (p. 337) and
is isoelectronic with the P, molecule, is also
appropriate.

‘There are three general methods for forming
Si-C bonds. The most convenient laboratory
method for small-scale preparations is by the
reaction of SiCl, with organolithium, Grignard
or organoaluminium reagents. A second attractive
route is the hydrosilylation of alkenes, ic.
the catalytic addition of Si-H across C=C
double bonds; this is widely applicable except
for the crucially important methyl and phenyl
silanes. Industrially, organosilanes are made by
the direct reaction of RX or ArX with a
fluidized bed of Si in the presence of about
10% by weight of metallic Cu as catalyst (cf,
the direct preparation of organo compounds of

tive since there is 1:1 cotrespondence between

electronic O- in the silicates (see p. 366). This
reminds us of the essentally covalent nature of
the Si -O-Si linkage, but the analogy should not
be taken ro imply idemity of structures in detail,
panicularly for the more highly condensed poly:
mers. Some aspects ofthe technology of silicones

Germanium, Tin and Lead

10.1 Introduction

Germanium was predicted asthe missing
element of a triad between silicon and_ tin
by J.A.R. Newlands in 1864, and in 1871
D.1. Mendeleev specified the properties that
“ekasilicon” would have (p.29). The new
element was discovered by C. A. Winkler in
1886 during the analysis of a new and rare
mineral argyrodite, AgsGeS¢;"") he named it in
honour of his country, Germany.? By contrast,
tin and lead are two of the oldest metals known

    

1M. E. WaIKs, Discovery ofthe Elements, 6th e¢n., Journal
‘of Chemical Education Publ. 1956, 910 pp. Germanium,
0p 683-98 Tin and lea, pp. 41-7
‘The astonishing corespondence between the predicted and
‘observed properties of Ge (p. 29) has tempted later writers 10
‘overlook the fact that Winkler thought he had discovered a
‘metalloid like As and Sb and he originally identified Ge with,
“Mendeleev's incorrectly) predicted “eka-stibium” between
‘Sb and Biz Mendeleev himself thought it was “eki-cadmium",
which he had (egain incorteily) predicted as a missing
clement between Cd ard Hg. H. T. von Richter thought it
‘was “ek-slicon”: so did Lothar Meyer, and they proved 10
be conect. This illustrates the great difficulties encountered
by chemists working only 100 y ago, yet three decades before
the rationale which stemmed from the work of Moseley and
Bote.

er

to man and both are mentioned in early books
of the Old Testament. The chemical symbols
for the elements come from their Latin names
stannum and plumbur. Lead was used in ancient
Egypt for glazing pottery (7000-5000 Bc); the
Hanging Gardens of Babylon were floored with
sheet lead to retain moisture and the Romans used
lead extensively for water-pipes and plumbing:
they extracted some 6-8 million tonnes in four
centuries with a peak annual production of
60.000 tonnes. Production of tin, though equally
influential, has been on a more modest scale and
dates back t0 300~3200.8c. Bronze weapons
and tools containing, 10-15% Sn alloyed with Cu
have been found at Ur, and Pliny described solder
as an alloy of Sn and Pb in AD 79.

Germanium and Sn are non-toxic (like C and
Si). Lead is now recognized as a heavy-metal
poison;®) it acts by complexing with oxo-groups

224, 15-78 971), Reprimed as Chap. 36 in Chembiry
1m the Emironment, Readings trom Scientific. American

368 Germanium, Tin and Lead

in enzymes and affects virtually all steps in
the process of haem synthesis and porphyrin
metabolism. It also inhibits acetylcholine-
esterase, acid phosphatase, ATPase, carbonic
anhydrase, ete. and inhibits protein synthesis
probably by modifying transfer-RNA. In addition
to O complexation (in which it resembles TI,
Bal! and Ln'"!), pb! also inhibits SH enzymes
(though less strongly than Cd" and Hg"),
especially by interaction with cysteine residues
in proteins. Typical symptoms of lead poisoning
are cholic, anaemia, headaches, convulsions,
chronic nephritis of the kidneys, brain damage
and central nervous-system disorders. Treatment
is by complexing and sequestering the Pb
using a strong chelating agent such as edta,
{-CH2N(CHCO>H)>}2, or BAL ie. British anti-
Lewisite, HSCH)CH(SH)CH?OH.

10.2 The Elements
10.2.1 Terrestrial abundance and
distribution

Germanium and Sn appear about half-way down
the list of elements in order of abundance in
crustal rocks, together with several other elements
in the region of 1-2ppm:

Element Br U Sn Eu Be As

PPM 25 23 2 21 2 18
Order 46 47 «48 -=48— 50

Element Ta Ge Ho Mo W = Tb
PPM. 17 4S 14 12 12 12
Order a i

Germanium minerals are extremely rare but the
element is widely distributed in trace amounts
(like its neighbour Ga). Recovery has been
achieved from coal ash but is now normally
from the fiue dusts of smelters processing Zn
ores.

 

Arsenic in the Environment, SCOPE 31, Wiley, Chichester,
1987, 384 pp.

Ch. 10

Tin occurs mainly as cassiterite, SnO2, and
this has been the only important source of
the element from earliest times. Julius Caesar
recorded the presence of tin in Britain, and
Comwall remained the predominant supplier
for European needs until the present century
(apart from a minor flourish from Bohemia
between 1400 and 1550).) Today (1990s) world
production approaches 200 000 tonnes per annum
(see next section), of which the UK contributes
Jess than 1%.)

Lead (13 ppm) is by far the most abundant of
the heavy elements, being approached amongst
these only by thallium (8.1 ppm) and uranium
(23ppm). This abundance is related to the
fact that 3 of the 4 naturally occurring
isotopes of lead (206, 207 and 208) arise
primarily as the stable end products of the
natural radioactive series. Only ™Pb (1.4%)
is non-radiogenic in origin. The variation in
isotopic composition of Pb with its origin
also accounts for the variability of atomic
weight and the limited precision with which
it can be quoted (p. 19). The most important
Pb ore is the heavy black mineral galena,
PbS. Other ore minerals are anglesite (PbSO,),
cerussite (PbCOs), pyromorphite (Pbs(POs)sC1)
and mimetesite (Pbs(AsO,)3Cl). Some 25 other
minerals are known but are not economically
important; all contain Pb! in contrast to tin
minerals which are invariably Sn'¥ compounds.
Lead ores are widely distributed and commercial
deposits are worked in over fifty countrie:
Primary production (from mines) was 3.3 million
tonnes (as Pb) in 1991 of which four-fifths
came from the half dozen main producers:
Australia 17.4%, USA 14.3%, the former Soviet
Union 13.8%, China 9.6%, Canada 8.3% and
Peru 6.0%. Secondary production (from the
resmelting of scrap) produces a further 5.6Mtpa
ie nearly two-thirds of the world’s supply
in 1991. The average price in 1992 was
£306.4/tonne ($542/),

§10.2.2

10.2.2 Production and uses of the
elements

Recovery of Ge from flue dusts is complicated,
not only because of the small concentration of
Ge but also because its amphoteric properties
are similar to those of Zn from which it
is being separated.) Leaching with H2SO,,
followed by addition of aqueous NaOH, results
in the coprecipitation of the 2 elements at
pH ~5 and enrichment of Ge from ~2 to
10%: GeO, begins to precipitate at pH 2.4, is
90% precipitated at pH 3, and 98% precipitated
at pH5. Zn(OH)2 begins to precipitate at
pH 4 and is completely precipitated at pH 5.5.
The concentrate is heated with HCVCl to
drive off GeCls, bp 83.1° (cf. ZnCl, bp 756°).
After further fractionation of GeCly, hydrolysis
affords purified GeO, which can be slowly
reduced to the element by Hp at ~530°.
Final purification for semiconductor-grade Ge is
effected by zone refining. World production of
Ge in 1991 was 80000kg (80tonnes), about
10% less than a decade earlier. The largest
use is in transistor technology and, indeed,
transistor action was first discovered in this
element (p. 331). This use is now diminishing
somewhat whilst that in optics is growing — Ge
is transparent in the infrared and is used in
infrared windows, prisms and lenses. Magnesium
germanate is a useful phosphor, and other
small-scale applications are in special alloys,
strain gauges and superconductors. Despite its
spectacular increase in availability during the past
few decades from a laboratory rarity to a general
article of commerce Ge and its compounds are
still relatively expensive. Zone-refined Ge was
quoted at $850 perkg in 1991 and GeO, at
$500 per kg.

‘The ready reduction of SnO2 by glowing coals
accounts for the knowledge of Sn and its alloys
in the ancient world. Modern technology uses
a reverberatory furnace at 1200-1300°. The
main chemical problem in reducing SnO7 comes

5 Kirk-Othmer Encyclopedia of Chemical Technology 4th
edn. 12, 540-55 (1994). Germanium and germanium
‘compounds,

Production and uses of the elements 369

from the presence of Fe in the ores which leads
to a hard product with unacceptable properties.
Reference to Ellingham-type diagrams of the
sort shown on p. 308 shows that ~AG(SnO2)
is very close to that for FeO/Fes0, and only
about 80kJ mol"! above the line for reducing
FeO to Fe at 1000-2000". It is therefore essential
to reduce cassiterite/iron oxide ores at an oxy-
gen pressure sufficiently high to prevent exten-
sive reduction to Fe. This is achieved in a two-
stage process, the impure molten Sn from the
initial carbon reduction being stirred vigorously
in contact with atmospheric O> to oxidize the
iron — a process that can be effected by “poling”
with long billets of green wood — or, alterna-
tively, by use of steam or compressed air. The
price of tin was formerly regulated by The Inter-
national Tin Council, but the market became
progressively less stable and the suspension of
buffer stock interventions in October 1985 pre-
cipitated on immediate collapse in the market
from which it has not yet recovered. The ITC was
superseded by The Association of Tin Produc-
ing Countries which attempts to limit production
by the member countries. In 1991 there was an
excess of tin on the world market for the 11th
successive year and primary production was lim-
ited to 95850tonnes (Malaysia 29.8%, Indone-
sia 29.6%, Thailand 17.9%, Bolivia 13.2%,
Australia 7.2%, Zaire 1.4%, Nigeria 0.9%). Add
tional production by China (43000 1), the for-
mer USSR (13 500 t) and other countries brought
the primary production of Sn in concentrates in
1991 to 196700 tonnes. Prices hovered around
$5700 per tonne, about half that of a few years
earlier”) The many uses of metallic tin and its
alloys are summarized in the Panel overleaf.
Lead is normally obtained from PbS. This is
first concentrated from low-grade ores by froth
flotation then roasted in a limited supply of air

Germanium, Tin and Lead Ch. 10

‘Uses of Metallic Tin and Its Alloys

Because of its low strength and high cost, Sn is seldom used by itself but its use as a coating, and as alloys, is familiar
in a variety of domestic and technological applications. Tin-plate accounts for almost 27% of tin used — it provides a
‘non-toxic corrosion-resistant cover for sheet steel and can be applied either by hot dipping in molten Sn or more elegantly
and controllably by electrolytic tinning. The layer is typically 0.4~25 um thick. In addition to extensive use in food
Packaging, tin-plate is used increasingly for distributing beer and other drinks. In the USA alone 35000 million of the
130000 million drink cans sold annually are tin-plate, the rest being Al: this is a staggering per capita consumption of

S00pa.

‘The main alloys of tin together with an indication of the percentage of total Sn production for these alloys in the USA

(1991) are:
Solder (37%)

Bronze (7%)

Babbitt (2%)

Pewter (3%)

(Su/Pb) typically containing 33% Sn by weight but varying between 2~63% depending
‘on use; sometimes Cd, Ga, In or Bi are added for increased fusibility

(Cu/Sn) typically 5~10% Sn often with added P or Zn to aid casting and impart superior
elasticity and strain resistance. Gun metal is ~85% Cu, 5% Sn, 5% Zn and 5% Pb.
Coinage metal and brass also often contain small amounts of Sn. World production of
‘bronzes approaches 500000 tonnes pa.

(heavy duty bearing metal introduced by I. Babbitt in 1839). The two main compositions
are 80-90% Sn, 0-5% Pb, 5% Cu; and 75% Pb, 12% Sn, 13% Sb, 0-1% Cu. They
have the characteristics of a hard compound embedded in’a soft matrix and are used
mainly in railway wagons, diese! locomotives, etc.

(00-95% Sn, 1-8% Sb, 0.5-3% Cu); a decorative and servicable alloy that can be cast,
bent, spun or formed into any shape; it is much used for coffee and tea services, trays,
plates, jugs, tankards, candelabra, bowls and trophies. A related alloy of 90-95% Sn
with Pb and other elements is highly prized and much used for organ pipes because of its

tonal qualities, e.g. the Royal Albert Hall organ in London has 10.000 pipes containing,
some 150 tonnes Sn.

Other specialized uses of Sn and its alloys are as type metal, as the molten-metal bath in the manufacture of float
lass and as the alloy Nb3Sa in superconducting magnets. The many industrial and domestic uses of tin compounds are

<iscussed in later sections; these compounds account for about 15% of the tin produced worldwide.

to give PbO which is then mixed with coke and

° Kirk-Othmer Encyclopedia of Chemical Technology 4th
edn. 15, 69-113 (1995), Lead; 113-32, Lead alloys; 132-58,
Lead compounds.

In either case the Pb contains numerous
undesirable metal impurities, notably Cu, Ag,
Au, Zn, Sn, As and Sb, some of which are
clearly valuable in themselves. Copper is first
removed by liquation: the Pb bullion is melted
and held just above its freezing point when Cu
rises to the surface as an insoluble solid which is
skimmed off. Tin, As and Sb are next removed by
preferential oxidation in a reverberatory furnace
and skimming off the oxides; alternatively, the
molten bullion is churnéd with an oxidizing flux
of molten NaOH/NaNO3 (Harris process). The
softened Pb may still contain Ag, Au and perhaps
Bi. Removal of the first two depends on their
preferential solubility in Zn: the mixed metals are
cooled slowly from 480° to below 420° when the
Zn (now containing nearly all the Ag and Au)
solidifies as a crust which is skimmed off; the

---
# Page 393

§10.2.3

Uses of Lead Alloys and Chemicals

Although much lead is used as an inert material in cast, rolled or extruded form. a far greater tonnage is consumed
as alloys. Its major application is in storage batteries where an alloy of 91% Pb, 9% Sb forms the supporting grid for
the oxidizing agent (PbO2) and the reducing agent (spongy Pb).") Over 70% of this Pb is recovered and recycled, In
addition, its use (with Sn) in solders, fusible alloys, bearing metals (babbit) and type metals has been summarized on
p. 370, Other mechanical as distinct from chemical applications are in ammunition, lead shot, lead weights and ballast.

The patter of chemical usage of Pb compounds in a particular country depends very much on whether organolead
‘compounds are allowed as antiknock additives in petrol for cars (gasoline for automobiles). In a growing number of
developed countries such additives are considered to be wasteful, dangerous and unnecessary and environmental legislation
is gradually achieving the elimination of PEW and PbMes as antiknocks.” The presence of Pb additives in petrol also
interferes with the catalytic converters that are being developed to reduce or eliminate CO, NO, and hydrocarbons from
exhaust fumes, and this has likewise encouraged the change to other antiknocks.

‘World production of mined lead was 3331 000tonnes in 1991 and a further 5 558 000tonnes was refined by reprocessing.
In the same year US consumption of Pb in metal products was | 125000tonnes (including 967 000tonnes in storage
batteries). In addition, some 57 250tonnes of other oxides and 29750tonnes of miscellaneous Pb-containing products
were consumed. The 'US market price of Pb dropped from $1.05/Kg in 1990 to $0.40/kg in 1993 due in part to the
collapse in use of PbEt, in petrol

Lead pigments are widely used as rustinbibiting priming paints for iron and steel, Red lead (PbyOs) is the traditional
primer but CazPbOs is finding increasing use, particularly for galvanized steel. Lead chromate, PbCrOs, is strong
yellow pigment extensively used in yellow paints for road markings and as an ingredient (with iron blues) in many green
paints and coloured plastics. Other pigments include PbMoO« (red-orange), litharge PbO (canary yellow), and white
lead, ~2PbCOs.PO(OH)>. Lead compounds are also used for ceramic glazes, c.g, PbSi2Os (colourless), in crown glass
‘manufacture, and as polyvinylebloride plastic stabilizers, eg. “wibasic lead sulfate", 3PbO.PbSO4.H2O. See also p. 386.

 

 

Properties of the elements 371

excess of dissolved Zn is then removed either
by oxidation in a reverberatory furnace, or by
preferential reaction with gaseous Cl;, or by
vacuum distillation, Final purification (which also
removes any Bi) is by electrolysis using massive
cast Pb anodes and an electrolyte of acid PbSiF,
or a sulfamate; this yields a cathode deposit of
99.99% Pb which can be further purified by zone
refining to <1 ppm impurity if required. Total
world production figures and the current price
were given at the end of the preceding section,
and the various uses for lead alloys and chemicals,
are summarized in the Panel

 

10.2.3 Properties of the elements

The atomic properties of Ge, Sn and Pb are
compared with those of C and Si in Table 10.1.
Trends noted in previous groups are again
apparent. The pairwise similarity in the ionization
energies of Si and Ge (which can be related to
the filling of the 3d! shell) and of Sn and Pb

"AT. KUHN (ed.), The Electrochemistry of Lead,
Academic Press, London, 1977, 467 pp. H. Bove, Lead-Acid
Batteries, Wiley. New York, 1977, 408 pp.

(which is likewise related to the filling of the 4f"*
shell) are notable (Fig. 10.1). Tin has more stable
isotopes than any other element (why?) and one
of these, '!Sn (nuclear spin }), is particularly
valuable both for nmr experiments!) and for
Méssbauer spectroscopy. (!?)

Some physical properties of the elements are
compared in Table 10.2. Germanium forms brit-
tle, grey-white lustrous crystals with the diamond
structure; it is a metalloid with a similar electri-
cal resistivity to Si at room temperature but with
a substantially smaller band gap. Its mp, bp and
associated enthalpy changes are also lower than
for Si and this trend continues for Sn and Pb
which are both very soft, low-melting metals.

Tin has two allotropes: at room temperature
the stable modification is white, tetragonal

10.2.4 Chemical reactivity and group
trends

Germanium is somewhat more reactive and more
electropositive than Si: it dissolves slowly in hot
concentrated H2SO, and HNOs but does not react
with water or with dilute acids or alkalis unless
an oxidizing agent such as HO, or NaOC! is
present; fused alkalis react with incandescence to
give germanates. Germanium is oxidized to GeO>
in air at red heat and both HS and gaseous S
yield GeS2; Clp and Br2 yield GeX, on moderate
heating and HCI gives both GeCly and GeHCh.
Alkyl halides react with heated Ge (as with
Si) to give the corresponding organogermanium
halides.

Tin‘) is notably more reactive and electropos-
itive than Ge though it is still markedly ampho-
teric in its aqueous chemistry. It is stable towards
both water and air at ordinary temperatures but
reacts with steam to give SnOz plus Ho and
with air or oxygen on heating to give SnO2.
Dilute HCI and HSO, show little, if any, reac-
tion but dilute HNO; produces Sn(NO3)2 and
NHNOs. Hot concentrated HCI yields SnClz
and Hz whereas hot concentrated H2SO, forms
SnSO, and SOz. The occurrence of Sn! com-
pounds in these reactions is notable. By con-
trast, the action of hot aqueous alkali yields

 

 

'3 P, G, HaRrison (ed.), Chemistry of Tin, Blackie, Glasgow,
1989, 461 pp.

hydroxostannate(IV) compounds, e.g,
Sn + 2KOH + 4H,0 —- Ko[Sn(OH),] + 2H2

Tin reacts readily with Clp and Br) in the cold
and with F; and Ip on warming to give SnX,. It
reacts vigorously with heated $ and Se, to form
Sn" and Sn'¥ chalcogenides depending on the
proportions used, and with Te to form SnTe.
Finely divided Pb powder is pyrophoric but the
reactivity of the metal is usually greatly dimin-
ished by the formation of a thin, coherent, pro-
tective layer of insoluble product such as oxide,
oxocarbonate, sulfate or chloride. This inertness
has been exploited as one of the main assets of
the metal since early times: e.g. a temperature
of 600-800" is needed to form PbO in air and
Pb is widely used for handling hot concentrated
H,SO,. Aqueous HCI does, in fact, react slowly
to give the sparingly soluble PbC1, (<1% at room
temperature) and nitric acid reacts quite rapidly to
liberate oxides of nitrogen and form the very sol-
uble Pb(NO3)2 (~50g per 100cm*, ie. 1.5 M).
Organic acids such as acetic acid also dissolve
Pb in the presence of air to give Po(OAc)2, etc.;
this precludes contact with the metal when pro-
cessing or storing wine, fruit juices and other
drinks. The familiar soft metal protective caps
covering the cork on quality wines is Pb-foil lam-
inated between thin outer layers of non-toxic Sn
metal to which coloured decorative finishes can
be applied. Fluorine reacts at room temperatures
to give PbE2 and Cl gives PbCl: on heating,

---
# Page 396

374 Germanium, Tin and Lead

Molten Pb reacts with the chalcogens to give PbS,
PbSe and PbTe.

‘The steady trend towards increasing stability of
Mt" rather than MY compounds in the sequence
Ge, Sn, Pb is an example of the so-called “‘inert-
pair effect” which is well established for the
heavier post-transition metals. The discussion on
p. 226 is relevant here. A notable exception is
the organometallic chemistry of Sn and Pb which
is almost entirely confined to the M!Y state
(pp. 399-405).

Catenation is also an important feature of the
chemistry of Ge, Sn and Pb though less so than
for C and Si. The discussion on p. 341 can be
extended by reference to the bond energies in
Table 10.3 from which it can be seen that there
is a steady decrease in the M-M bond strength.
In general, with the exception of M-H bonds,
the strength of other M~X bonds diminishes less
noticeably, though the absence of Ge analogues
of silicone polymers speaks for the lower stability
of the Ge~O~Ge linkage.

‘The structural chemistry of the Group 14
elements affords abundant illustrations of the
trends to be expected from increasing atomic
size, increasing electropositivity and increasing
tendency to form M" compounds, and these will
become clear during the more detailed treatment
of the chemistry in the succeeding sections

compounds (Which arises from the presence of a
nonbonding electron-pair on the metal) is

particularly revealing as also is the propensity

ability of both Sn and Pb to form polyatomic
cluster anions of very low formal oxidation
state, (e.g. Ms?-, Mo‘, etc.) reflects the now
well-established tendency of the heavier post-
transition elements to form chain, ring or

established for the polyhalide anions and for

10.3 Compounds

10.3.1 Hydrides and hydrohalides

Germanes of general formula Ge,Hz»42 are
known as colourless gases or volatile liquids
for m=1-5 and their preparation, physical
properties, and chemical reactions are very
similar to those of silanes (p. 337). Thus GeHy
was formerly made by the inefficient hydrolysis
of Mg/Ge alloys with aqueous acids but is now
generally made by the reaction of GeCl; with
LiAIHg in ether or even more conveniently by
the reaction of GeO, with aqueous solutions
of NaBH. The higher germanes are prepared
by the action of a silent electric discharge on
GeH,; mixed hydrides such as SiHjGeH can
be prepared similarly by circulating a mixture
of SiH, and GeH, but no cyclic or unsaturated
hydrides have yet been prepared. The germanes
are all less volatile than the corresponding
silanes (see Table) and, perhaps surprisingly,

and SnHz, GeHy does not ignite in contact with

aqueous NaOH. It acts as an acid in liquid NH
forming NH,* and GeH3~ ions and reacts with
alkali metals in this solvent (or in MeOC2Hy-
OMe) to give MGeHs. Like the corresponding
MSIHs, these are white, crystalline compounds
of considerable synthetic utility. X-ray diffraction
analysis shows that KGeH; and RbGeHs have
the NaCl-type structure, implying free rotation
of GeHs”, and CsGeHs has the rare TH structure
(p. 242). The derived “ionic radius” of 229pm

from broad-line nmr experiments: SiH3~ 94+ 4°
(cf. isoelectronic PHs, 93.5°); GeH3~ 92.5 + 4°
(Cf. isoelectronic AsHs, 91.8").

‘The germanium hydrohalides GeH,X4_. (X =
Cl, Br, Ik x= 1, 2, 3) are colourless, volatile,
reactive liquids. Preparative routes include reac-
tion of Ge, GeX2 or GeHy with HX. The
compounds are valuable synthetic intermedi-
ates (cf. Sis]. For example, hydrolysis of
GeHLCl yields O(GeHs)2, and various metathe-
ses can be effected by use of the appro-
priate Ag salts or, more effectively, Pb!
salts, e.g. GeH3Br with PbO, Pb(OAc):, and
Pb(NCS)p affords O(GeHs)2, GeH(OAc), and
GeH(SCN). Treatment of this latter compound
with MeSH or (Mn(CO)sH] yields GeHsSMe

and [Mn(CO)s(GeH)] respectively. An extensive
phosphinogermane chemistry is also known, e.g.

Binary Sn hydrides are much less stable.
Reduction of SnCl, with ethereal LiAIHy gives
SnH, in 80-90% yield; SnCl; reacts similarly
with aqueous NaBH. SnHy (mp— 146", bp
—52.5°) decomposes slowly to Sn and Hp at
room temperature; it is unattacked by dilute
aqueous acids or alkalis but is decomposed
by more concentrated solutions. It is a potent
reducing agent. SnzHg is even less stable, and
higher homologues have not been obtained. By
contrast, organotin hydrides are more stable,
and catenation up to H(SnPh2)sH has been
achieved by thermolysis of PhSnH,. Preparation
of R,SnHy_» is usually by LiAlH, reduction of
the corresponding organotin chloride.

PbH; is the least well-characterized Group 14
hydride and it is unlikely that it has ever been
prepared except perhaps in trace amounts at high
dilution; methods which successfully yield MH;
for the other Group 14 elements all fail even at
low temperatures. The alkyl derivatives R>PbH2
and R3PbH can be prepared from the correspond-
ing halides and LiAIH, at —78° or by exchange
reactions with Ph3SnH, e.g.:

BujPbX + Ph3SnH —> Bu}PbH + Ph3SnX
Me;PbH (mp ~ 106", decomp above —30°)
and Et;PbH (mp ~—145*, decomp above
20") readily add to alkenes and alkynes

(hydroplumbation) to give stable tetraorganolead
compounds.

Germanium tetrahalides are readily prepared
by direct action of the elements or via the action
of aqueous HX on GeO>. The lighter members
are colourless, volatile liquids, but Gels is an
orange solid (cf. CX4, SiX;). All hydrolyse read-
ily and GeCl, in particular is an important inter-
mediate in the preparation of organogermanium
compounds via LiR or RMgX reagents. Many
mixed halides and hydrohalides are also known,
as are complexes of the type GeFg?~, GeCl,?-,

from the 4 basal F atoms which are at 181, 195,
220, and 245 pm from the apical Ge"; the Gel¥
atom is at the centre of a slightly distorted octahe-
dron (GelY-F 171-180pm, angle F-Ge-F
87.5-92.5°) and the whole structure is held
together by F bridges.

Tin halides

The structural chemistry of Sn! halides is
particularly complex, partly because of the

2 5. C. Tavuor and P, W. WILSON, J. Amt Chem. Soc. 95,
1834-8 (1973),

Halides and related complexes 37

stereochemical activity (or non-activity) of the
nonbonding pair of electrons and partly because
of the propensity of Sn"! to increase its CN by
polymerization into larger structural units such
as rings or chains. Thus, the first and second
ionization energies of Sn (p. 372) are very similar
to those of Mg (p. 111), but Sn” rarely adopts
structures typical of spherically symmetrical ions
because the nonbonding pair of electrons, which
is 5s? in the free gaseous ion, readily distorts
in the condensed phase; this can be described in
terms of ligand-field distortions or the adoption
of some “p character”. Again, the “nonbonding”
pair can act as a donor to vacant orbitals, and the
“vacant” third Sp orbital and Sd orbitals can act
as acceptors in forming further covalent bonds.
A good example of this occurs with the adducts
[SnX2(NMes)] (X= Cl, Br, 1): the Sn! atom,
which has accepted a pair of electrons from the
ligand NMes, can itself donate its own lone-
pair to a strong Lewis acid to form a double
adduct of the type [BF3{<SnX2(<-NMes)}] (X =
Cl, Br, 1). Further examples, including more
complicated interactions, are described later in
this subsection.

SnF; (which is obtained as colourless
monoclinic crystals by evaporation of a solution
of SnO in 40% aqueous HF) is composed of
‘SnjFs tetramers interlinked by weaker Sn-F
interactions; the tetramers are puckered 8-
membered rings of alternating Sn and F as
shown in Fig. 10.3 and each Sn is surrounded
by a highly distorted octahedron of F (1
Sn-F, at ~205pm, 2 Sn-F, at ~218pm,
and 3 much longer Sn---F in the range
240-329 pm, presumably due to the influence
of the nonbonding pair of electrons. In
aqueous solutions containing F~ the predominant
species is the very stable pyramidal complex
‘SnF3~ but crystallization is attended by further
condensation. For example, crystallization of
SnF; from aqueous solutions containing NaF
does not give NaSnF3 as previously supposed but

Apart from its structural interest, SnCl, is
important as a widely used mild reducing agent
in acid solution. The dihydrate is commercially
available for use in electrolytic tin-plating baths,
asa sensitizer in silvering mirrors and in the
plating of plastics, and as a perfume stabilizer
in toilet soaps. The anhydrous material can
be obtained either by dehydration using acetic

‘SnBr2 is a white solid when pure (mp 216°,
bp 620°); it has a layer-lattice structure but the
details are unknown. It forms numerous hydrates
(e.g. 3SnBr).H;0, 2SnBr2.H20, 6SnBr2.5H20)
all of which have a distorted trigonal prism
of 6 Br about the Sn! with a further Br and
H2O capping one or two of the prism faces
and leaving the third face uncapped (presumably
because of the presence of the nonbonding
pair of electrons in that direction).°) A
similar pseudo-9-coordinate structure is adopted
by 3PbBr.2H,0. By contrast, NH,SnBr;.H2O
adopts a structure in which Sn" is coordinated by
a tetragonal pyramid of 5 Br atoms which form
chains by edge sharing of the 4 basal Br; the
Sn’ is slightly above the basal plane with Sn~Br
304-350 pm and Sn—Brazex 269 pm. The NHy*
and H,0 form rows between the chains.

Snl forms as brilliant red needles (mp 316”,
bp 720°) when Sn is heated with Ip in 2M
hydrochloric acid. It has a unique structure in
which one-third of the Sn atoms are in almost per-
fect octahedral coordination in rutile-like chains
(2 Sn-1 314.7 pm, 4 Sn—I 317.4 pm, and no sig-
nificant distortions of angles from 90°); these
chains are in turn cross-linked by double chains
containing the remaining Sn atoms which are
themselves 7-coordinate (5 Sn-I all on one
side at 300.4~325.1 and 2 more-distant I at
371.8pm).) There is an indication here of
reduced distortion in the octahedral site and this
has been observed more generally for compounds
with the heavier halides and chalcogenides in
which the nonbonding electron pair on Sn" can
delocalize into a low-lying band of the crystal.
Accordingly, SnTe is a metalloid with cubic NaCl
structure. Likewise, CsSn"Brs has the ideal cubic
perovskite structure (p. 963);°5) the compound
forms black lustrous crystals with a semi-metallic

about Sn: the (SnFs} units are joined into planar
layers by edge-sharing of 4 equatorial F atoms
(Sn-F,, 202 pm) leaving 2 further (terminal) F in
trans positions above and below each Sn (Sn-Fy
188 pm). The other SnX, can be made by direct
action of the elements and are unremarkable
volatile liquids or solids comprising tetrahedral
molecules. Similarities with the tetrahalides
of Si and Ge are obvious. The compounds
hydrolyse readily but definite hydrates can also
be isolated from acid solution, e.g. SnCly.5H,0,
SnBry.4H,0. Complexes with a wide range
of organic and inorganic ligands are known,
particularly the 6-coordinate cis- and trans-
LySnXz and occasionally the 1:1 complexes
LSnX,. Stereochemistry has been deduced
by infrared and Mossbauer spectroscopy and,
when possible, by X-ray crystallography. The
octahedral complexes SnXs’- (X = Cl, Br, I)
are also well characterized for numerous cations.
Five-coordinate trigonal bipyramidal complexes
are less common but have been established for
SnCls~ and MeSnCl;~. A novel rectangular
pyramidal geometry for Sn'V has been revealed
by X-ray analysis of the spirocyclic dithiolato
complex anion [(MeCgH3S2),SnCI]~: the Cl
atom occupies the apical position and the Sn
atom is slightly above the plane of the 4 S atoms
(mean angle Cl-Sn-Cl 103°).°7) A. similar
stereochemistry has also been established for Si¥
(p. 335) and for Gel¥ in ((CsH4O2),GeCl]~.

 

 

Lead halides

Lead continues the trends outlined in preceding
sections, PbX2 being much more stable thermally
and chemically than PbX,, Indeed, the only stable
tetrahalide is the yellow PF, (mp 600°); PbCl,
is a yellow oil (mp —15°) stable below 0° but
decomposing to PbCl and Cl; above 50°; PbBry
is even less stable and Pbl, is of doubtful exis-
tence (cf. discussion on THs, p. 239). Stability
can be markedly increased by coordination: e.g,

37. C. Sau, R.O. Day and R.R, HOLMES, Inorg. Chem.
20, 3076-81 (1981); J. Am. Chem. Soe. 102, 7972-3 (1980),

---
# Page 404

382 Germanium, Tin and Lead

direct chlorination of PbCl; in aqueous HCI fol-
lowed by addition of an alkali metal chloride
gives stable yellow salts MzPbCls (M = Na, K,
Rb, Cs, NHj) which can serve as a useful source
of Pb!Y. By contrast, PbX2 are stable crystalline
compounds which can readily be prepared by
treating any water-soluble Pb! salt with HX or
halide ions to precipitate the insoluble PbX2. As
with Sn, the first two ionization energies of Pb
are very similar to those of Mg; moreover, the
6-coordinate radius of Pb" (119 pm) is virtually
identical with that of Sr’ (118 pm) and there is
less evidence of the structurally distorting influ-
ence of the nonbonding pair of electrons. Thus
@-PbF2, PbClz, and PbBr2 all form colourless
orthorhombic crystals in which Pb" is surrounded
by 9 X at the corners of a tricapped trigonal
prism. There are, in fact, never 9 equidistant X
neighbours but a range of distances in which one
can discern 7 closer and 2 more distant neigh-
bours. This (7 + 2)-coordination is also a feature
of the structures of BaX2 (X = Cl, Br, D), EuCl,
CaHp, etc.; see also hydrated tin(Il) bromides,
p. 380.

‘The high-temperature f-form of PbF2 has
the cubic fluorite (CaF;) structure with 8
coordinated Pb! Pbl, (yellow) has the Cdl;
hexagonal layer lattice structure. Like many
other heavy-metal halides, PbCl, and PbBr,
are photo-sensitive and deposit metallic Pb on
irradiation with ultraviolet or visible light. PbI>
is a photoconductor and decomposes on exposure
to green light (Amgx 494.9nm). Many mixed
halides have also been characterized, e.g. PbECI,
PbEBr, POFI, PbX2.4PbF>, etc. Of these PbFCI
is an important tetragonal layer-lattice structure
type frequently adopted by large cations in the
presence of 2 anions of differing size;“* its
sparing solubility in water (37mg per 10cm?
at 25°C) forms the basis of a gravimetric method
of determining F. It is also interesting to note
that PbF, was the first ionically conducting
crystalline compound to be discovered (Michael
Faraday, 1838).

GeO is obtained as a yellow sublimate when
powdered Ge and GeO> are heated to 1000",
and dark-brown crystalline GeO is obtained
on further heating at 650°. The compound
can also be obtained by dehydrating Ge(OH)>

Characterized. Both are reducing agents and GeO
disproportionates rapidly 10 Ge and GeO above
700°. Much more is known about GeO; and there
an impressive resemblance between the oxide
chemistry of Gel¥ and Si. Thus hexagonal
GeO, has the 4-coordinated P-quartz structure
(p. 342), tetragonal GeO has the 6-coordinated
rutile-like structure of stishovite (p. 343), and
vitreous GeO; resembles fused silica. Similarly,
Ge analogues of all the major types of silicates
and. aluminosilicates. (pp. 347-59) have been
prepared, BeyGeOs and ZmGeO, have the
(Phenacite and willemite structures with “isolated”
{GeO4) units; SeaGezO, has the thortveitite
structure; BaTiGe3Os has the same type of eyclic
ion as benitoite, and CaMgGe0¢ has a chain
structure similar to diopside. Further, the two
crystalline forms of CazGeOs are isostructural
‘with two forms of CaySiOe, and CayGeOs
crystallizes in no fewer than 4 of the known
structures of CasSiOs. The reaction chemistry of
the two sets of compounds is also very similar.

386 Germanium, Tin and Lead

The Oxides of Lead?

PbO (ted, orange or yellow depending on the method of preparation) is amphoteric and dissolves readily in both acids and
alkalis, I is much used in glass manufacture since a high Pb content leads to greater density, lower thermal conductivity,
higher refractive index (greater brilliance), and greater stability and toughness. The replacement of the very mobile alkali
‘ons by Pb also leads to high electrical capacities, comparable with mica. PbO is also used to form stable ceramic glazes
and vitreous enamels (see SnO>, p. 385). Electric storage batteries are the other major user of PbO (either as litharge or
as “black oxide", ie. PbO + Pb). The plates of the battery consist of an inactive grid or support onto which is applied
a paste of PbO/H2SOs. The positive plates are activated by oxidizing PbO to PbO» and the negative plates by reducing
PbO to Pb. Another use of PbO is in the production of pigments (p. 371).

Red lead (Pb3O4) is manufactured on the 20000-tonne scale annually and is used primarily as a surface coating to
prevent corrosion of iron and stcel (check oxidation-reduction potentials). It is also used in the production of leaded
glasses and ceramic glazes and. very substantially, as an activator, vulcanizing agent and pigment in natural and artificial
rubbers and plastics.

PbO; is a strong oxidizing agent and, in addition to its in situ production in storage batteries, itis independently
manofactured for use as an oxidant in the manufacture of chemicals, dyes, matches and pyrotechnics. It is also used

considerable quantity as a curing agent for sulfide polymers and in high-voltage lightning arrests, Because of the
instability of Pb! PbO» tends to give salts of Pb!! with liberation of O2 when treated with acids, e.g.

 

 

 

PbO: + HzSO, “> PbSOs + H20+ $02 *

PbO2 + 2HNO; ——+ Pb(NOs)2 +H20+ $02

‘Warm HCI reacts similarly but in the cold POCl, is obtained. PbO? is produced commercially by the oxidation of PbyO4
in alkaline slury with Clz and the technical product is marketed in 0-kg drums.

Mixed oxides of Pb!Y with other metals find numerous applications in technology and industry. They are usually made
by beating PDO> (or POO) in ar with the appropriate oxide, hydroxide or oxoacid sl, the product formed being dependent
‘on the stoichiometry used, e.g. M"Pb!YOs, MUPBIVO, (Mi! = Ca, Sr, Ba). CaPbOs in particular is increasingly replacing
bs04 as a priming pigment to protect steel against corrosion by salt water. Mixed oxides of Pb! are also important.
Ferrimagnetic oxides of general formula PbO.nFez03 (n = 6, 5, 25, 1, 0.5) can be prepared by direct reaction but
have not proved to be as attractive, commercially. asthe hard ferite BaFey20}9. By contrast, the ferroelectric behaviour
(©. 57) of several mixed oxides with Pol! has excited considerable interest. Many of these compounds have a distorted
perovskite-type structure (p. 963); e.g yellow PBTiOs (Ferroelectric below 490°C), colourless PbZrO (230°), and PDHfO3
QIS*, antiferroelectric). Others have a tetragonal tungsten-bronze-type structure (p. 1016), e.g. PbNb2O¢ (Ferroelectric up
{0 560°C), PbTi2Og (~215"). The mode of action and uses of hard ferroelectrics has been discussed on p. 58, and the
high Curie temperature of many Po! ferroelectrics makes them particularly useful for high-temperature applications.

# Page 409

§10.3.4

of O atoms in the (001) direction is missing
(p. 383), ie. [PbzsO24(C_)rs]; it therefore seems
reasonable to suppose that the anion vacancies
in [Pb24039(C_)io} are also confined to alternate

should show no variability in composition. Fur-
ther heating above 350° (or careful oxidation
of PbO) yields Pb;2O17 (i.e. PbO} 437) which is
also a stoichiometric ordered defect fluorite struc-
ture [Pb>4O3s(C1_)4). However, oxidation of
phase under increasing oxygen pressure leads to
@ nonstoichiometric phase of variable composi-
tion between PbO} 42 and PbO} 57 in which there
appears to be a quasi-random array of anion
vacancies.)

Lead does not appear to form a simple
hydroxide, Pb(OH):, [cf. Sn(OH),, p. 384].
Instead, increasing the pH of solutions of Pb!
salts leads to hydrolysis and condensation, see
[PbsO(OH),]** (p. 395).

    

  

 

 

10.3.4 Derivatives of oxoacids

Oxoacid salts of Ge are usually unstable, gen-
erally uninteresting, and commercially unimpor-
tant. The tetraacetate Ge(OAc)s separates as
white needles, mp 156°, when GeCl, is treated
with TIOAc in acetic anhydride and the result-
ing solution is concentrated at low pressure and
cooled. An unstable sulfate Ge(SO,)> is formed
in a curious reaction when GeCl, is heated with
SO; in a sealed tube at 160°:

GeCl, + 680; —> Ge(SOs), + 2520sClr

Numerous oxoacid salts of Sn! and Sn'¥
have been reported and several basic salts are
also known. Anhydrous Sn(NO3)2 has not been
prepared but the basic salt Sn3(OH)s(NOs)2
can be made by reacting a paste of hydrous
tin(I1) oxide with aqueous HNOs; the com-
pound may well contain the oligomeric cation
[Sn3(OH)4]** illustrated on p. 384. Sn(NO3)s
can be obtained in anhydrous reactions of

455, S, ANDERSON and M. STERNS, J. Inorg. Nucl. Chem. 11,
272-85 (1959),

Derivatives of oxoacids 387

SnCl, with NOs, CINOs or BrNOs; the com-
pound readily oxidizes or nitrates organic com-
pounds, probably by releasing reactive NOs
radicals. Many phosphates and phosphato com-
plexes have been described: typical examples
for Sn" are Sn3(POs)2, SnHPO,, Sn(HzPO,)2,
Sn2P20; and Sn(POs)2. Examples with Sn'¥
are SmO(PO,)2, Sn;0(POs)2.10H20, SnPsO;,
KSn(POs)3, KSnOPO, and Na;Sn(PO,)2. One
remarkable compound is tin(IV) hypophosphite,
Sn(HpPO,), since it contains Sn! in the pres-
ence of the strongly reducing hypophosphorous
anion; it has been suggested that the isolation of
Sn(H2PO>), (colourless crystals) by bubbling O,
through a solution of SnO in hypophosphorous
acid, (HyPO(OH)], may be due to a combination
of kinetic effects and the low solubility of the
product.

‘Treatment of SnO; with hot dilute HySOx yields
the hygroscopic dihydrate Sn(SO4)2.2H20. In the
Sn" series SnSOj is a stable, colourless compound
which is probably the most convenient laboratory
source of Sn! uncontaminated with Sn'V.
readily prepared by using metallic Sn to displace
Cu from aqueous solutions of CuSOs. SnSO,
‘was at one time thought to be isostructural with
BaSO, but this seemed unlikely in view of the
very different sizes of the cations and the known
propensity of Sn to form distorted structures;
now known to consist of (SOs) groups linked into
a framework by O-Sn-O bonds in such a way
that Sn is pyramidally coordinated by 3 O atoms
at 226 pm (O-Sn-O angles 77~79°); other Sn-O
distances are much larger and fall in the range
295-334 pm. A basic sulfate and oxosulfate
are also known:

Most oxoacid derivatives of lead are Pb"
compounds, though Pb(OAc), is well known and
is extensively used as a selective oxidizing agent
in organic chemistry.) It can be obtained as,

48K. C, Momtoy, K. QuiLL, D. CUNNINGHAM, P, MCARDLE
and T, HIGGINS, J. Chem. Soc., Dalton Trans., 267 73 (1989),
R.N, BUTLER, in J. S. PEY (ed.), Synthetic Reagents,

Ch. 10

colourless, moisture-sensitive crystals by treating
Pb;Os with glacial acetic acid. Pb(SO,) is
also stable when dry and can be made by
the action of conc H2SO, on Pb(OAc); or
by electrolysis of strong HoSO, between Pb
electrodes. PbSO, is familiar as a precipitate
for the gravimetric determination of sulfate
(solubility 4.25mg per 10cm? at 25°C); PbSeOs
is likewise insoluble. By contrast Pb(NO3)> is
very soluble in water (37.7g per 100cm’ at
0°, 127g at 100°). The diacetate is similarly
soluble (19.7 and 221 g per 100cm’ at 0° and 50°
respectively). Both compounds find wide use in
the preparation of Pb chemicals by wet methods
and are made simply by dissolving PbO in the
appropriate aqueous acid. A large number of
basic nitrates and acetates is also known. The
thermal decomposition of anydrous Pb(NOs)>
above 400° affords a convenient source of N2Ox
(see p. 456).

Other important Pb" salts are the carbonate,
basic carbonate, silicates, phosphates and per-
chlorate, but little new chemistry is involved.
PbCO; occurs as cerussite; the compound is
made as a dense white precipitate by treating
the nitrate or acetate with CO, in the presence
of (NHy)2CO3 or NaxCOs, care being taken to
keep the temperature low to avoid formation of
the basic carbonate ~2Pb(CO3).Pb(OH)2. These
compounds were formerly much used as pig-
ments (white lead) but are now largely replaced
by other white pigments such as TiO? which
has higher covering power and lower toxicity.
The highly soluble perchlorate [and even more
the tetrafluoroborate Pb(BFs)2) are much used as
electrolytic plating baths for the deposition of
Pb to impart corrosion resistance or lubricating
properties to various metal parts. Throughout the
chemistry of the oxoacid salts of Pb" the close
correlation between anionic charge and aqueous
solubility is apparent.

‘The complex coordination chemistry of Pb" is
also beginning to be actively explored and some
unusual stereochemistries are emerging. Thus, the
mononuclear (n?-nitrato)bis(phenanthrolene)(N-
thiocyanato) complex _ (Pb(phen)s(NCS)(n?-
NO;)] has 7-coordinate Pb! with a large vacancy

---
# Page 411

§10.3.5

in the coordination sphere, possibly indicating
‘a stereochemically active lone pair.“ Again,
{Pb(phen),(OCIOs)|C1O, features 9-fold, capped
square antiprismatic coordination about Pb,'*")
whereas in (Pbltpy)s]{CIO.}> (tpy = 2, 2:62"
terpyridine) there is an unusual D3 9-coordinate
environment around the Pb" centre.*?)

10.3.5 Other inorganic compounds

Few of the many other inorganic compounds of
Ge, Sn and Pb call for special comment. Many
pseudo-halogen derivatives of Sn'Y, Pb' and Pb!
have been reported, e.g. cyanides, azides, iso-
cyanates, isothiocyanates, isoselenocyanates and
alkoxides.)

All 9 chalcogenides MX are known (X = S,
Se, Te). GeS and Sn$ are interesting in having
layer structures similar to that of the isoelectronic
black-P (p. 482). The former is prepared by
reducing a fresh precipitate of GeS2 with excess
HPO, and purifying the resulting amorphous
red-brown powder by vacuum sublimation. SnS.
is usually made by sulfide precipitation from Sn™
salts. PbS occurs widely as the black opaque
mineral galena, which is the principal ore of Pb
(p. 368). In common with PbSe, PbTe and SnTe,
it has the cubic NaCl-type structure. Pure PbS
can be made by direct reaction of the elements or
by reaction of Pb(OAc)2 with thiourea; the pure
compound is an intrinsic semiconductor which,
in the presence of impurities or stoichiometric
imbalance, can develop either n-type or p-
type semiconducting properties (p. 332). It is
also a photoconductor (like PbSe and PbTe)

 

 

SLM. ENGELHARDT, JM. Parkick and A. H. WHITE,
Aust. J. Chem. 42, 335-8 (1989). See also L. M. Exoe.’
HaRDT, B.M. FuRPHY, J. McB. HARROWFIELD, J. M. PAT
Rick, B.W. SKELTON and A. H.WaITE, J. Chem. Soc.,

5). W. ApeL, Tin, Chap. 17 in Comprehensive Inorganic

Other inorganic compounds 389

and is one of the most sensitive detectors
of infrared radiation; the photovoltaic effect
in these compounds is also widely used in
photoelectric cells, e.g. PbS in photographic
exposure meters. The three compounds are also
unusual in that their colour diminishes with
increasing molecular weight: PbS is black, PbSe
grey, and PbTe white.

Of the selenides, GeSe (mp 667°) forms as a
dark-brown precipitate when H2Se is passed into
an aqueous solution of GeCl). SnSe (mp 861°)
is a grey-blue solid made by direct reaction of
the elements above 350°. PbSe (mp 1075°) can
be obtained by volatilizing PbCl: with H2Se, by
reacting PbEU, with H2Se in organic solvents, or
by reducing PbSeO, with Hz or C in an elec-
tric furnace; thin films for semiconductor devices
are generally made by the reaction of Pb(OAc);
with selenourea, (NH2),CSe. The tellurides are
best made by heating Ge, Sn or Pb with the
stoichiometric amount of Te.

Other chalcogenides that have been described
include GeSp, GeSe2, SnzS3 and SnSe, but
these introduce no novel chemistry or structural
principles. Of more interest, perhaps, is the
polymeric anion [Sns$}2~Joo (1) which occurs
in CssSns§j2.2H20 and which contains both
trigonal bipyramidal and octahedral Sn'¥,)

afforded Rb2Sn3S7.2H20 in which the polymeric
[Sn3S;? Joo anion (2) features both SnS;
tetrahedra and SnSg octahedra.°) Another
new structural form, in which a commo-
Sn atom joins a double cube, is found in
the discrete (Sn7SsQ2} cluster core (3) of
{{Bu'Sn(S)L}s]2Sn; the diphosphinate ligand L =

atoms in each of the cubes.‘ Examples of

reaction of SnCl; with 2Li{C(PMe,)s} in Ftz0 at

and axial Sn-P distances (260pm vs 279 and

angle of 62.9° at the Sn atom. The compound

axial and equatorial positions. Several similar
compounds are known.') Germanium analogues

‘A range of shiny metallic compounds featuring
trigonal planar anions SnX;* (X= As, Sb,
Bi) have been characterized with composition
M(SnX3)Oo.s (M=Rb, Cs); the Sn and X
atoms in SnX35~ (isostructural with CO3?~) are
coordinated by trigonal prisms of 6M*, and the
0 ions occupy octahedral sites in the M*
lattice.)

Rather different is the X-ray structural
characterization of the ‘bare’ Sn’* ion in
[Sn?*][SbF,~]2.2AsF3 (prepared by treating the
product of the direct reaction between SnF and
‘SbF; with AsF;).!)) The crystal packing is such
that each Sn?+ is surrounded by nine F atoms
(tricapped trigonal prism) and the average Sn—F
distance is 257pm (cf. the sum of the ionic
radii, 251 pm). The Mssbauer spectrum (p. 371)
shows zero quadrupole splitting and the highest
known chemical shift for any tin(II) species,
consistent with the ‘bare ion’ formulation.

3GeClp.diox + 4Li[{C(PMe2)2X}2] = (7) + (8)

where X+SiMe3 (or PMe2). The Ge-Ge
distance in (8) is 254 pm, i.e, about 10pm longer
than in polygermanes. The stereochemically
active lone pairs of electrons on Gel in
(8) can be used as electron-pair donors
to a further GeCl moiety to form the
homonuclear (germanediyl donor) ~(germanediyl
acceptor) complex [Ge2 j1-(PMe,)xCX}2]2GeCl,
which features a mixed-valent Ges chain
as shown schematically in (9). The Ge-Ge
distances along the Ge!-Ge!—Gel!-Ge!—Ge!
chain are 249.2, 255.4, 256.2 and 248.5 pm,
respectively.

{Nb(OEt)s}4).

 

10.3.7 Organometallic compounds ©

Germanium ®”

Organogermanium chemistry closely resembles
that of Si though the Ge compounds tend
to be somewhat less thermally stable. They
are also often rather more chemically reactive
than their Si counterparts, eg. in ligand
scrambling reactions, Ge-C bond cleavage and
hydrogermylation. However, GeRs compounds
themselves are rather inert chemically and
R,GeXs-n tend to be less prone to hydrolysis
and condensation reactions than their Si
analogues. Again, following expected group
trends, germylenes (R,Ge:) are more stable
than silylenes. The table of comparative bond
energies on p. 374 indicates that the Ge-C and
Ge-H bonds are weaker than the corresponding,
bonds involving Si but are nevertheless quite
strong; Ge-Ge is noticeably weaker. The
electronegativity of both Si and Ge are similar to
that of H, though the reactivity pattern towards
organolithium reagents suggests a slight hydridic
character (H’-) for PhsSiH and some. protic
character (H'*) for Ph3GeH:

Ph3Si-H + Lik —> Ph3Si-R + Li
(metathesis)

Ph;Ge-H + Lik ——> PhsGe-Li + RH
(metallation)

 

°C. ExscMBNBROICH and A. SALZER, Organometallics,
VCH, Weinheim, 1989, pp. 115-46,

7p. Rivibee, M. RIVIBRE-BaUDer and J. Sarat, Chap. 10
in G. WILKINSON, F.G. A. Sroxe and E. W. Apel. (eds.)
Comprehensive Organometallic Chemistry, Pergamon Press,
‘Oxford, Vol. 2, pp. 399-518 (1982) (716 refs.).

Ch. 10

In fact, the polarity of the Ge-H bond can readily
be reversed (umpolung) by an appropriate choice
of constituents, e.g.:

Bn Get-HY + >C=0 —> H-C-OGeBty
(germoxane)
I
ChGe“-HY + >C=0 —> ChGe-C-OH

(germylearbinol)

Preparative routes to organogermanium com-
pounds parallel those for organosilicon com-
pounds (p. 363) and most of the several thou-
sand known organogermanes can be considered
as derivatives of R,GeX4-» or AryGeX4- where
X = hydrogen, halogen, pseudohalogen, OR, etc.
The compounds are colourless, volatile liquids,
or solids. Attempts to prepare (-RxGeO-), ana-
logues of the silicones (p. 364) show that the
system is different: hydrolysis of MexGeCh is
reversible and incomplete, but extraction of aque-
ous solutions of Me2GeCl: with petrol leads to
the cyclic tetramer [Me:GeO],, mp 92°; the com-
pound is monomeric in water. Organodigermanes
and -polygermanes have also been made by stan-
dard routes, e.g.

 

2MesGeBr + 2k “3 2kBr + Ge>Mes
(mp —40°, bp 140°)
lig NH
Et:GeBr + NaGePh; ——— NaBr
+ EtyGeGePh3 (mp 90°)

boiling.
2Ph;GeBr + 2Na —> 2NaBr + Ge2Pho

xylene
(mp 340°)
PhyGeCh + 2NaGePh; ——+ 2NaCl + GesPhs
(mp 248°)
In general, the Ge~Ge bond is readily cleaved
by Br2 either at ambient or elevated temperatures
but the compounds are stable to thermal cleavage
at moderate temperatures. Ge>Rg compounds can
even be distilled unchanged in air (like SizRg but
unlike the more reactive Sn Rg) and are stable
towards hydrolysis and ammonolysis.

$10.3.7

Considerable recent attention has focused
‘on the preparation, structure and stability of
germenes (>Ge=C<), germylenes (R,Ge:),
cyclo and polyhedral oligopolygermanes, and
Gel" species with coordination numbers greater
than 4 (especially 5 and 10). Thus, evidence
for fugitive germene species has been known
for some 20 years but stable germenes,
R,Ge=CR’, were first reported only in
1987, the stabilization being achieved by
use of bulky groups both on Ge [e.g.R=
mesityl or -N(SiMe3)] and on C [e.g. R}
~B(Bu')C(SiMes),B(Bu')— or CR, = fluorenyl-
dene}. Numerous other stable germenes have
since been characterized.°

‘The first germylene, R>Ge: [R = (SiMes)2-
CH-], was reported in 1976. It can now
be conveniently prepared from GeCl:.diox
and Grignard-type derivatives of the bulky
bis(trimethylsilyl)methyl R group in EO (eg.
ether complexes of RMgCl or MgR2); gas-
phase electron diffraction at 155°C shows it
to be a V-shaped monomer with the angle
CGeC 107°." In the solid phase the compound
forms bright yellow crystals (mp 182°C) of
the centrosymmetric dimer Ge>R, which has a
trans-folded framework (see structure on p. 403)
with a fold angle 6 of 32° and a Ge-Ge
distance of 235 pm.” By contrast, reductive
coupling reactions of RxGeX; with a mixture
of Mg/MgBr) in thf affords colourless crystals
of cyclotrigermanes or cyclotetragermanes in
moderate or good yield:°)
