Disulfur decafluoride, S2Fyo, is obtained as a
byproduct of the direct fluorination of sulfur to
SF¢ but is somewhat tedious to separate and is

more conveniently made by the photolytic reduc-
tion of SCIFs (prepared as above):

bw
2SCIF, + Hz —> S2Fjo + 2HCI

It is intermediate in reactivity between SF, and
the very inert SF. Unlike SF, it is not hydrolysed
by water or even by dilute acids or alkalis and,
unlike SF, itis extremely toxic. It disproportion
ates readily at 150° probably by a free radical
mechanism involving SFs" (note the long, weak
S bond; Fig. 15.20):

 

150"
S2Fo —> SF; +SFe

Similarly it reacts readily with Cl) and Br) to
give SCIFs and SBrFs. It oxidizes KI (and I~) in
acetone solution to give iodine (note SF, converts
acetone to Me,CF2). S2Fio reacts with SO> to
give FsSSO2F and with NH to give N=SF}.

Sulfur hexafluoride is unique in its stability
and chemical inertness: it is a colourless,
odourless, tasteless, unreactive, non-flammable,
non-toxic, insoluble gas prepared by burning
sulfur in an atmosphere of fluorine. Because of
its extraordinary stability and excellent dielectric
properties it is extensively used as an insulating
gas for high-voltage generators and switch
gear: at a pressure of 2-3 bars it withstands
1.0-1.4MV across electrodes SOmm apart
without breakdown, and at 10 bars it is used for
high-power underground electrical transmission
systems at 400V and above. However, there
is now some environmental concern at its use
as an electrical transformer fluid and as an
inert blanketing gas in magnesium metal casting,
since even minute amounts may contribute to an
atmospheric greenhouse effect (it is 6800 times
‘as potent as COs).

SF can be heated to 500° without decomposi-
tion, and is unattacked by most metals, P, As, etc.,
even when heated. It is also unreactive towards

---
# Page 704

688 Sulfur

high-pressure steam presumably as a result of
kinetic factors since the gas-phase reaction SF +
3H,0 —+ SO; +6HF should release some
460kJ mol! (AG° ~ 200kJ mol” '). By contrast,
reaction with HS yields sulfur and HF, Hot
HCI and molten KOH at 500° are without
effect. Boiling Na attacks SF, to yield NaS
and NaF; indeed, this reaction can be induced
to go rapidly even at room temperature or below
in the presence of biphenyl dissolved in glyme
(J,2-dimethoxyethane). It is also reduced by
Na/liq NH and, more slowly, by LiAlHW/Et0.
Al;Cl at 200° yields AIF;, Cly, and sulfur chlo-
rides. Recent experiments‘? indicate that SF5
becomes much more reactive at higher temper-
atures and pressures; for example PF; is quan-
titatively oxidized to PFs at 500° and 300 bars,
and to a mixture of PFs and SPF; at ~380° and
1800-3600 bars.

Also notable are sulfur cyanide fluorides such
as SF\CN,‘“®) SF(CN)p("") and SFsCN(4 42)

Chlorides, bromides and iodides of sulfur

Sulfur is readily chlorinated by direct reaction
with Cl but the simplicity of the products
obtained belies the complexity of the mechanisms
involved. The reaction was first investigated by
C. W. Scheele in 1774 and has been extensively
studied since because of its economic importance
(see below) and its intrinsic physicochemical
interest. Direct chlorination of molten $ followed
by fractional distillation yields disulfur dichloride
(SClp) a toxic, golden-yellow liquid of revolting
smell: mp —76°, bp 138°, d(20°) 1.677 gem~*
The molecule has the expected C2 structure
(like SoF2, H2O2, etc.) with S-S 195 pm,
S-C1 206pm, angle Cl-S-S 107.7", and a
dihedral angle of 85.2°.("") Further chlorination
of S,Clp, preferably in the presence of a trace
of catalyst such as FeCls, yields the more-
volatile, cherry-red liquid sulfur dichloride, SCly:
mp —122°, bp 59°, d(20°) 1.621 gcem™. SC
resembles S,Cly in being foul-smelling and toxic,
but is rather unstable when pure due to the
decomposition equilibrium 2SClh — $,Ch +
Clz. However, it can be stabilized by the presence
of as little as 0.01% PCls and can be purified
by distillation at atmospheric pressure in the
presence of 0.1% PCls.{') The sulfur dichloride
molecule is nonlinear (C,) as expected, with
S-CI 201 pm and angle Cl-S-C1 103°.

SoClz and SCly both react readily with
H2O to give a variety of products such as

HpS, SOz, H2S03, H2SO4 and the polythionic
acids H2S,O¢. Oxidation of SCh yields thionyl
chloride (OSClz) and sulfuryl chloride (O2SC12)
(see Section 15.2.4). Reaction with F; produces
SF, and SF (p. 686), whereas fluorination with
NaF is accompanied by some disproportionation:

3SCly + 4NaF —> SF, +S2Cl, +4NaCl

As indicated on p. 686, fluorination of SClz with
KF/SO, occurs with concurrent isomerization
to SSF, Both S;Cly and SC react with
atomic N (p. 413) to give NSCI as the first
step, and this can then react further with
S2Cly to give the ionic heterocyclic compound
S3N2CI*CI~ (p. 739). By contrast, reaction of
S2Ch with NH,C1 at 160° (or with NH3 + Cl in
boiling CCl) yields the cluster compound S4Ny
(p. 722). Treatment of S2Cl, with Hg(SCN)>
yields colourless crystals of S4(CN)s, mp —2°,
which are composed of unbranched chain
molecules NCSSSSCN with essentially linear
NCS groups (177.5°, 178.4°) and the angles CSS
98.6° and SSS 106.5°; interatomic distances are
within the expected ranges, viz. N=C 113.4,
C-S 169.6, outer S-S 206.8 and inner S—S
201.7pm."45) SCI, acts as a ligand to Pd and
Pt in the yellow 4-coordinate complex trans-
[PdCl,(SCl,)>] and the red 6-coordinate complex
trans-[PtCl(SClz)2].'% These are formed when
either Pd or Pt metal is heated in a quartz ampoule
with elemental S and Cl; at 200°C for 4 days, and.
they decompose into SCl; and PdCl; or PtCly,
respectively, on being heated.

S,Ch_ and SClz are important industrial
chemicals. The main use for S2Clz is in the
vapour-phase vulcanization of certain rubbers,
but other uses include its chlorinating action in
the preparation of mono- and di-chlorohydrins,
and the opening of some minerals in extractive
metallurgy. Some idea of the scale of production,
can be gauged from the fact that SClz is shipped
in 50-tonne tank cars; smaller quantities are
transported in drums containing 300 or 60kg.

of the liquid. Its less-stable homologue SCly
is notable for its ready addition across olefinic
double bonds: e.g., thiochlorination of ethene
yields the notorious vesicant, mustard gas:

SCh +2CH;=CH, —+ S(CH;CH2C)),

The compounds SCl; and $C, can be thought
of as the first two members of an extended
series of dichlorosulfanes S,Clz. The lower
electronegativity of Cl (compared with F) and
the lower S-Cl bond energy (compared with
S-F) enable the natural catenating propensity
of S to have full reign and a series of
dichlorosulfanes can be prepared in which S~S
bonds in sulfur chains (and rings) can be broken
and the resulting -S,— oligomers stabilized by
the formation of chain-terminating S-C1 bonds.
The first eight members with n =1—8 have
been isolated as pure compounds, and mixtures

up to perhaps SyCh are known.t Specific
compounds have been made by F. Fehér's group
using the polysulfanes as starting materials
(p. 683):047)

HS, + 282Cl, —+ 2HCI + S44xClo
80°
HS, + 2SCl —+ 2HCI + S244Ch.

The dichlorosulfanes are yellow to orange-yellow
viscous liquids with an irritating odour. They
are thermally and hydrolytically unstable. SsClz
boils at 31° (10~' mmHg) and has a density of
1.744 gcm™> at 20°. Higher homologues have

The higher chlorides of S (unlike the
higher fluorides) are very unstable and poorly
characterized. There is no evidence for molecular
chloro analogues of SF,, S2Fjo and SFs, though
SCIFs is known (p. 687), Chlorination of SClz
by liquid Cly at —78° yields a powdery off-
white solid which begins to decompose when
warmed above —30°. It analyses as SCl, and
is generally formulated as SCl;*CI-, but little
reliable structural work has been done on it.
Consistent with this ionic formulation, reaction of
SCI, with Lewis acids results in the formation of
stable adducts; e.g. AICI; yields the white solid
SCl,.AICI; which has been shown by vibrational
spectroscopy on both the solid and the melt
(125°) to be [SCl;]*[AICL,]}~." The compound
[SCl]*[ICIz] is also known (p. 693). As
expected from a species that is isoelectronic
with PCI; the cation is pyramidal; dimensions
are: S-Cl (average) 198.5 pm, angle Cl-S-Cl
101.3° (cf. PCls: P-Cl 204.3 pm, angle Cl-P-CI
100.1°). Other compounds containing [SCl]*
which have been characterized spectroscopically
and by X-ray crystallography include those with
[SbClg]~, [UCIg}> and [AsFe}~.1%

Sulfur bromides are but poorly characterized
and there are few reliable data on them. SBr2
probably does not exist at room temperature but
has been claimed as a matrix-isolated product
when a mixture of $2Cl/SClx:Br2:Ar in the ratio
1:1:150 is passed through an 80-W microwave
discharge and the product condensed on a CsI

Sulfur iodides are a topic of considerable
current interest, although compounds containing
S-I bonds were, in fact, unknown until fairly
recently. The failure to prepare sulfur iodides
by direct reaction of the elements probably
reflects the comparative weakness of the S-I
bond: an experimental value is not available but
extrapolation from representative values for the
bond energies of other S-X bonds leads to a
value of ~170 kJ mol~!:

 

 

Bond S-F S-Cl S-Br SI S-S 11
Energy/

Kimol-! 327 271 218 (~170) 225 150

The data indicate that formation of Sly
from }S3 +2 and the formation of S2I) from
48s +b are both endothermic to the extent of
~35kJ mol”, implying that successful synthesis
of these compounds must employ kinetically
controlled routes to obviate decomposition back
to the free elements.

We conclude this section with an amusing
cautionary tale which illustrates the type of
blunder that can still appear in the pages of a
refereed journal (1975) when scientists (in this

 

 

SSG. A. BowmaxeR and D. A. RoceRs, J. Chem. Soc.
Dalton Trans., 1146-51 (1981).

8° R. Minkwrrz and H. PRENZEL, Z. anorg. alle. Chem. $48,
91-102 (1987).

Oxohalides of sulfur 693

case physicists) attempt to deduce the structure
of a compound by spectroscopic techniques
alone, without ever analysing the substance being
investigated. The work!" purported to establish
the presence of a new molecule ClsSI in solid
solution with an ionic complex [SCls]*fICh}-,
thus leading to an overall formula for the crystals
of $2Clgl2. The mixed compound had apparently
been made originally by M. Jaillard in 1860:
he obtained it as beautiful transparent yellow-
orange prismatic crystals by treating a mixture
of sulfur and iodine with a stream of dry Clz
R. Weber obtained the same material in 1866
by passing Cl; into a solution of Ip in CS2 but
he reported a composition of SChrI rather than
haillard’s SCI (S2Clgl2). The implausibility of
forming a stable compound containing an SI
bond in this way, coupled with the perceptive
recognition that the published Raman spectrum
had bands that could be assigned to [ICl4}~ rather
than [ICl]~, led P.N. Gates and A. Finch to
reinvestigate the compound. '6” It transpired that
the nineteenth-century workers had used S=16 as
the atomic weight of sulfur so the true chemical
composition of the crystals was, in fact, SClyI.
‘The previous spectroscopic interpretation!
was therefore totally incorrect and the compound
was shown to be [SCls]*{ICl,]~. This was later
confirmed by a single-crystal X-ray diffraction
study (p. 691).{"49) In short, far from containing
the new iodo-derivative ClsSI, the compound did
not even contain an S—I bond.

 

 

15.2.4 Oxohalides of sulfur

Sulfur forms two main series of oxohalides,
the thionyl dihalides OS'YX and the sulfuryl
dihalides O,S%'X2, In addition, various other
oxofluorides and peroxofluorides are known
(p. 688). Thionyl fluorides and chlorides are
colourless volatile liquids (Table 15.14); OSBr2
is rather less volatile and is orange-coloured.

All have pyramidal molecules (C, point group
for OSX2), and OSFCI is chiral though
stereochemically labile. Dimensions are in
Table 15.14: the short O-S distance is notable.
The unstable compound OSI; was mentioned on
p. 692.

The most important thionyl compound is
OSCl; — it is readily prepared by chlorination
of SOp with PCIs or, on an industrial scale, by
oxygen-atom transfer from SO3 to SClo:

SO> + PCl; —+ OSCl, + OPC],
S03 + SC —>+ OSC + S02

OSCl; reacts vigorously with water and is partic-
ularly valuable for drying or dehydrating readily
hydrolysable inorganic halides:

MX, .mH,0 + mOSCl, —+> MX, + mSO2
+ 2mHC1

Examples are MgCl;.6H20, AICI3.6H:0, FeCl;.-
64,0, etc. Thionyl chloride begins to decompose
above its bp (76°) into SCl, SO2, and Ch;
it is therefore much used as an oxidizing
and chlorinating agent in organic chemistry.
Fluorination with SbF;/SbFs gives OSF2; use
of NaF/MeCN gives OSFCI or OSF; according
to conditions. Thionyl chloride also finds
some use as a nonaqueous ionizing solvent
as does SO; (p.700) and the  forinally
related dimethylsulfoxide (dmso), Me»SO (mp
18.6°, bp 189°, viscosity 72s 1.996 centipoise,
dielectric constant 3s 46.7). OSF2 is a useful
low-temperature fluorinating agent in organic
chemistry: it converts active C-H and P-H

groups into C-F and P-F, and replaces N-H
with N—S(O)F.0@

Sulfuryl halides, like their thionyl analogues,
are also reactive, colourless, volatile liquids
or gases (Table 15.15). The most important
compound is OzSCl2, which is made on an
industrial scale by direct chlorination of SOz
in the presence of a catalyst such as activated
charcoal (p. 274) or FeCl. It is stable to 300°
but begins to dissociate into SO and Clz above
this: it is a useful reagent for introducing Cl
or O>SCI into organic compounds. O,SCl) can
be regarded as the acid chloride of H2SOq and,
accordingly, slow hydrolysis (or ammonolysis)
yields O2S(OH)2 or O,S(NH2)>. Fluorination
yields O,SF, (also prepared by SO2 + Fs) and
comproportionation of this with OxSClz and
O,SBr: yield the corresponding O,SFX species.

All these compounds have (distorted) tetrahedral

15.2.5 Oxides of sulfur

At least thirteen proven oxides of sulfur are
known to exist‘® though this profusion should
not obscure the fact that SO, and SO} remain
by far the most stable and unquestionably
the most important economically. The six
homocyclic polysulfur monoxides $,O(5 <n <
10) are made by oxidizing the appropriate
cyclo-S,, (p. 656) with trifluoroperoxoacetic acid,
CF,C(O)OOH, at —30°. The dioxides $;0> and
$60, are also known. In addition there are the
thermally unstable acyclic oxides $0, $02, SO
and the fugitive species SOO and SO,. Several
other compounds were described in the older
literature (pre-1950s) but these reports are now
known to be in error. For example, the blue
substance of composition “S203” prepared from
liquid SO; and sulfur now appears to be a mixture
of salts of the cations $2* and S,?* (p. 664) with
polysulfate anions. Likewise a “sulfur monoxide”
prepared by P. W. Schenk in 1933 was shown
by D. J. Meschi and R. J. Meyers in 1956 to be
a mixture of SO and SO2. The well-established
lower oxides of S will be briefly reviewed before
SO, and SOs are discussed in more detail.

Oxides of sulfur os

Lower oxides")

Elegant work by R. Steudel and his group in
Berlin has shown that, when cyclo-Sio, -So,
and -Sg are dissolved in CS; and oxidized by
freshly prepared CF;C(O)O;H at temperatures
below —10°, modest yields (10-20%) of the
corresponding crystalline monoxides S,O are
obtained. Similar oxidation of cyclo-S;, and a-
and f-Ss in CH2Ch solution yields crystalline
$0, $02, and a- and B-S6O. Crystals of S¢O2
and Ss (d > —50°) have not yet been isolated
but the compounds have been made in solution
by the same technique. $;0 had previously
been made (1972) by the reaction of OSCI,
and HS; in CS; at —40°: it is one of the
most stable compounds in the series and melts
(with decomposition) at 78°. All the compounds
are orange or dark yellow and decompose with
liberation of SO and sulfur when warmed to
room temperature or slightly above. Structures
are in Fig. 15.24. It will be noted that S;O
is isoelectronic and isostructural with [S7I]*
(p. 692). This invites the question as to whether
S)S can be prepared as a new structural isomer
of cyclo-Ss.

S30 reacts with SbCl; in CS, over a period

It will be noted that the SsO unit differs

and S-S interatomic distances. The X-ray
crystal structure was determined at —100°C
as the adduct decomposes within 5 min at
25° to give OSClz, SbCl; and Ss. When a
similar reaction was attempted with f-S.O, the
novel dimer S;202.2SbCls.3CS2 was obtained
as orange crystals in 10% yield after 1 week
at —50°65) (Fig. 15.25b). Formation of the
centrosymmetric S;202 molecule, which is still
unknown in the uncoordinated state, can be

# Page 715

§15.2.5 Oxides of sulfur 699

Much mote serious is the effect of volatile S compounds (mainly SO2) released into the atmosphere as @ result of
‘man’s domestic and industrial activities. This has been estimated to be some 200 million tonnes pa. and is comparable
in amount to all the sulfur released by natural processes (~310 x 10° tonnes pa). Unfortunately, by the very nature of
its origin, this $Op is released in the heart of densely populated areas and does great daninge 10 the respiratory organs
‘of man aid animals, to buildings, and perhaps mest seriously w plants, lake-waters and aquatic life as a result of

rain”. Dispersal by means of high chimney stacks is inadequate since this merely transfers the problem to neighbouring
regions. For example, only one-tenth of the serious $O2/H2S04 pollution of lakes and streams in Sweden is x8 a result
‘of atmospheric SO> emissions in Sweden itself; one-tenth is dve to emissions from the UK, and the remaining four-fifths
is from industrial regions in northem Europe.

Tn Europe and the USA (and presumably elsewhere) the major source of SO: pollution isn coal-based power generation;
this, ogether with other coal consumption and coking operations accounts for some 60% of the emissions. A further 25%
arises from cil-refinery operations, cil-ired power generation, and other ol consumption. Copper-smelting (together with
uch smaller amounts from zinc and lead ore processing) accounts for some 12% of the annual release of SO>. The sulfuric
acid manufacturing industry, which is the only one designed actually to make SQ2 on a large scale, only contributes <2%%
to the total, probably because of the efficient design of the process.

 

 

 

ATMOSPHERE OVER LAND. ATMOSPHERE OVER OCEAN

Figure A. The silfur budget for the land-atinosphere—ocean system. Annual turnover rates are indicated
in units of 10° tonnes (as estimated for 1977).

Ultimately, pollution can only be avoided by complete removal of SO> from the effluent gases, but this council
of perfection is both technologically and economically tnaitainable. Many processes are available 10 reduee the SO2
concentration to very low figures, but the vast scale of power generation and domestic heating by coal and cil still results
in substantial emission. SO> can be removed by scrubbing with a slury of “milk of lime", Ca(OH). Alternatively, partial
reduction (0 HS using natural gas (CHa), naphtha ot coal, followed by catalytic conversion to clemental sulfur by the
aus process can be used:

 

4 activated
21S + S02 Sas} +2420
BS + 501 384 2a

 

‘The detection of SO) in the atmosphere has becéme a refined analytical procedure. Several techniques are available
‘such as (a) absorption in aqueous H2O2 and titration (or conductimetric determination) of the resulting H2SO4 (H202 +
SO; ——+ H;S0q): and (b) reaction with NaglHgCly} or KalHeCik:

[gC +2502 +2120 —> [Hg(SO, 27 +4C1 44H
‘The resulting disulfitomercurate is detcrmined colorimetrically after addition of acidic pararosaniline and formaldehyde
(P. W, West and G. C. Gaeks, 1956). Other methods are (c) Hame-photometric monitoring of the gas stream usin
reducing H/ar ane and emission of $2 at 394 nt sensitive dovin wo | part in 10° by volume and (€) pulsed fluorescent
analyser using radiation in the region of 214 nm; this is specific for SO and response is linear over wide ranges down to
1 in 10°. Commercial instruments are available.

as a bleach, disinfectant (Homer, p. 645), food
preservative, refrigerant and nonaqueous solvent.
‘Other chemical uses are inthe preparation of sul-
fites and dithionites (p. 716) and, with Cl, in the
derivatization of hydrocarbons via sulfochlorina-
tion reactions. There is alo much current interest
in its properties as a multimode ligand (p. 701).
$02 is a colourless. toxic gas with a choking
‘dour. Maximuin permitted atmospheric concer
tration for humans is 5 ppm but many green plants
suffer severe distress in concentrations as low as
1-2ppm. SO: neither burns in air nor supports
combustion. Some molecular and physical prop-
erties of the compound are in Table 15.16, Com-
parison of these properties with those of ozone
(p. 607) is instructive. Note also that the S-O
distance of 143.1 pm in SOs is less than that
unstable SO (148.1 pm) whereas the O-O dis.
tance of 127.8 pm in Os is greater than that in sta
ble O: (120.7 pm), Furthermore the mean bond.
energy in SO is 548 KI mot! which is greater
than that for SO (524 kJ mol~') whereas the
rican bond energy in Ox is 297 kJ mot! which
is less than the value for O2 (490 kJ mot”), This
taken to imply an S~O bond order of at
‘SO: compared with only 1.5 for 0-0
in Ox (p, 6007),

By far the most important chemical reaction of
SO> is its further oxidation to SO; according to
the equilibrium

80:+ SOx: AH* = -98.6%) mo!
‘The equilibrium constant, Ky = p(SOs)/
Lp(50>).p! (O2)}, decreases rapidly with increas-
ing temperature: for example: log Kp = 3.49 at
‘800°C and -0.52 at 1100°C. Thus for maximum
oxidation during the manufacture of HaSOs itis
necessary 10 work at lower temperatures and 10
increase the rate of reaction by use of catalysts.

  

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  

 

 

   

 

‘Typical conditions would be to pass a mixture of
SO; and air over Pt gauze or more commonly a
V20s/K20 contact catalyst supported on Kiesel
guhr or zeolite.

Gaseous SO: is readily soluble in water
(3927 em" SO; in 100g H20 at 20°), Numerous
species are present in this aqueous solution
of “sulfurous acid” (p. 717), At O° a cubic
clathrate hydrate also forms with a composition
~SO;.6H,0; its dissociation pressure reaches
atm at 7.1°. The ideal composition would be
$02.53H20 (p. 627).

In addition (0 the role of gaseous SOz
in the manufacture of HzSO., pure (liquid)
SO; is manufactured on a large scale for the
tuses mentioned above. Typical production levels
(in 1985) were 162000 tonnes in USA and
£65000 tonnes in (West) Germany. About half of
this is used in the manufacture of S-containing
chemicals such as sulfites, hydrogen sulfites,
thiosulfates, dithionites, salts of hydroxalkane-
sulfinic acids and alkane sulfonates. I is also used
incellulose manufacture, in the chemical dressing
‘of Mn-ores, in the removal of S-containing,
impurities from mineral oils. for food disinfection
and preservation, and for treatment of water.

Liquid SO; has been much studied as a
nonaqueous solvent.!! Some of the early work
(particularly on the physical properties of the
solutions) is now known to be in error but

the solvent is especially useful for carrying
‘out a range of inorganic reactions. It is also
an excellent solvent for proton nmr studies. In
general, covalent compounds are very soluble:
eg. Br, ICI, OSX», BCs, CS2, PCls, OPCs and.
ASC; are completely miscible, and most organic
amines, ethers, esters, alcohols, mercaptans and
acids are readily soluble. Many uni-univalent
salts are moderately soluble, and those with
ions such as the tetramethylammonium halides
and the alkali metal iodides are freely so. The
low dielectric constant of liquid SO> leads to
extensive ion-pair and ion-triplet formation but
the solutions have limiting molar conductances
in the range 190-250ohm~! cm? mol~! at 0°.
Solvate formation is exemplified by compounds
such as SnBry.SO2 and 2TiCl,.SO, (see below
for SOp as a ligand). Solvolysis reactions are also
documented, e.g.:

NbCls + $02 —> NbOCI + OSChr
WC + $0. —> WOCL, + OSC
UCls + 280, —> U0,Cl, + 20SCh,

Several other reaction types have also appeared
in the literature but are sometimes purely for-
mal schemes dating from the time when the
solvent was (incorrectly) thought to undergo
self-ionic dissociation into SO? and SO3*~
or SO?* and S20s?~. More recently it has
been shown that, whereas neither SO; nor
SMe; (dmso) react with first-row transition
metals, the mixed solvent smoothly effects

 

dissolution of the metals with simultaneous
oxidation of S!Y to S¥!, thereby enabling the
production of crystalline solvated metal disul-
fates (S)072-) in high yield.'* Examples
are: colourless [Ti'¥(OSMe)6][S207]2, green
{VI"(OSMe>)s]21S207]s andthe salts
[M"(OSMe;).][S207], where M = Mn (yellow),
Fe (pale green), Co (pale pink), Ni (green), Cu
(pale blue), Zn (white) and Cd (white). This is
by far the most convenient way to prepare pure
disulfates. Dissolution of metals in SO> mixed
with other solvents such as dmf, dma, or hmpa
also occurs, but in these cases there is no oxi-
dation of the S'Y, and the product is usually the
metal dithionite, M!"[S204].

Sulfur dioxide as a ligand

The coordination chemistry of SO) has been
extensively studied during the past two decades
and at least 9 different bonding modes
have been established.("") These are illustrated
schematically in Fig. 15.26 and typical examples
are given in Table 15.17.'!®) It is clear
that nearly all the transition-metal complexes
involve the metals in oxidation state zero or +1.
Moreover, SO> in the pyramidal n!-clusters tends
to be reversibly bound (being eliminated when

‘Sulfur trioxide

‘SO is made on a huge scale by the catalytic
‘oxidation of S02 (p. 700): it is not usually
isolated but is immediately converted to H;SOx
(p. 708). It can also be obtained by the
thermolysis of sulfates though rather high
Temperatures are required. SO; is available
‘commercially as a liquid: such samples contain
small amounts (0.03 ~ 1.5%) of additives to inhibit
polymerization. Typical additives are simple
compounds of boron (e.g. B:s, B(OH)s, HBO>,
BX, MBF, NazB,0>), silica, siloxanes, SOCI>,
sulfonic acids, ete. The detailed mode of action
of these additives remains obscure. SOs is also
readily available as fuming sulfuric acid (or
foleum) which is a solution of 25-65% SOs
in HSOz (p. 707). Because of its extremely
aggressive reaction with most materials, pure
anhydrous SOy is difficult to handle although
it is made in the USA (for example) on a

 

 

Oxides of sulfur 703

scale approaching 90000 tonnes per annum. It
can be obiained on a laboratory scale by the
double distillation of oleum in an evacuated all-
lass apparatus; a small amount of KMaO. is
sometimes used to oxidize any traces of S03.

Tn the gas phase, monomeric SOs has a planar
(Diy) structure with $-O 142pm. This species
is in equilibrium with the cyclic trimer $:Oy
in both the gaseous and liquid phases: Ky ~
Vatm=? at 25%, SH" ~ 1251 (mole $xOy)~!,
Bulk properties therefore often refer to this
equilibrium mixture, e.g. bp 446°C, d(25°)
1.903 gcm"*, (25°). 1.820 centipoise. Below
the mp (16.86°), colourless crystals of ice-
like orthorhombic y-SOy separate and structural
studies reveal that the only species present is
the trimer $105 (Fig. 15.27), Traces of water
(10-" mole%) lead to the rapid formation of
listening, white, needle-like crystals of 6-SOs
which is actually & mixture of fibrous, polymeric
polysulfuric acids HO(SO30),H. where x is very
large (108), The helical chain structure of f+
0; is shown in Fig. 15.27 (cf. polyphosphates.
p. 528), A third and still more stable form, a-
SOs, also requires traces of moisture or other
polymerizing agent for its formation but involves
some cross-linking between the chains to give @
complex layer structure (mp 62°). The standard
enthalpies of formation (AHj/kI mol”) of the
various forms of SOs at 25°C are: gas ~395.2,
liquid -4379, y-crystals —447.4, Bocrystals
449.6. «solid ~462.4.

SO, reacts vigorously and extremely exother-
mically with water to give H2SOs. Substoichio-
metric amounts yield oleums and mixtures of
various polysulfuric acids (p. 712). Hydrogen
halides. give the corresponding halogenosulfu-
ric acids HSO3X, SOs extracts the elements of
HO from carbohydrates and other organic mvat-
ter leaving a carbonaceous char. It ucts as a
strong Lewis acid towards a wide varicty of
inorganic and organic ligands 10 give adducts
eg. onides give SO.2”, PhiP gives PhiP-SOs
(with a rather long P-'S bond, 217.6pmy"*

Ph3AsO gives Ph3AsO.SO; etc. Frequently fur-
ther reaction ensues: thus, under various con-
ditions reaction with NHs yields H2NSO3H,
HIN(SO3H)2, HN(SOsNHy)2, NH;N(SO3NH,)2,
etc. SO} can also act as a ligand towards strong,
electron-pair acceptors such as AsF;, SbF; and
SbCls. It is reduced to SO2 by activated char-
coal or by metal sulfides. The reaction with metal
‘oxides (particularly Fes0,) to give sulfates is
used industrially to rid stack-gases of unwanted
byproduct SOs,

  

Higher oxides

The reaction of gaseous SO2 or SOs with O2
in a silent electric discharge gives colourless
polymeric condensates of composition SOs,
(0 <x <1). These materials are derived from p-
SO5 by random substitution of oxo-bridges by
peroxo-bridges:

?
—0-$—0-0-S—0—
oO

Hydrolysis of the polymers yields H2SO, and
H,SOs (p. 712), with HO and Oz as secondary
products,

Monomeric neutral SO, can be obtained by
reaction of SOs and atomic oxygen; photolysis of
‘SOs/ozone mixtures also yields monomeric SOs,
which can be isolated by inert-gas matrix tech-
niques at low temperatures (15—78 K). Vibration
spectroscopy indicates either an open peroxo C,
structure or a closed peroxo C2, structure, the
former being preferred by the most recent study,
on the basis of agreement between observed and
calculated frequencies and reasonable values for
the force constants:("*5)

 

 

‘The compound decomposes spontaneously below
room temperature.

"SSP. La Bowviuue, R. KuoeL, and J. R. FERRARO, J. Chem.
Phys. 67, 1877-81 (1971).

15.2.6 Oxoacids of sulfur

Sulfur, like nitrogen and phosphorus, forms
many oxoacids though few of these can be
isolated as the free acid and most are known
either as aqueous solutions or as. crystalline
salts of the corresponding oxoacid anions.
Sulfuric acid, H2SOz, is the most important
of all industrial chemicals and is manufactured
‘on an enormous scale, greater than for any
other compound of any element (p. 407).
Other compounds, such as thiosulfates, sulfites,
disulfites and dithionites, are valuable reducing,
agents with a wide variety of applications.
Nomenclature is somewhat confusing but is
summarized in Table 15.18 which also gives
an indication of the various oxidation states
‘of S and a schematic representation of the
structures. Previously claimed species such as
“sulfoxylic acid” (HS0>), “thiosulfurous acid”
(HpS,02), and their salts are now thought not
to exist.

  

Sulfur

Many of the sulfur oxoacids and their salts
are connected by oxidation-reduction equilibria:
some of the more important standard reduction
potentials are summarized in Table 15.19 and
displayed in graphic form as a volt-equivalent
diagram (p. 435) in Fig. 15.28. By use of the
couples in Table 15.19 data for many other
oxidation-reduction equilibria can readily be
calculated. (Indeed, it is an instructive exercise
to check the derivation of the numerical data

# Page 723

iven in parentheses in Fig. 15.28 from the data
given in Table 15.19 and to calculate the standard
eduction potentials of other couples, e,
HSO,"/H,S 0.289V, HSO,"/H2S03 0.119.
HSOs/S203?- 0.433, ete.) Several important
points emerge which are immediately apparent
from inspection of Fig. 15.28. For example, it
is clear that, in acid solutions, the gradient
between Hz$ and Ss is less than between
Sg and any positive oxidation state, so that
HS is thermodynamically able to reduce any
oxoacid of sulfur to the element. Again,
as all the intermediate oxoacids lie above
the line joining HS, and Sp, it follows
that all can_ ultimately disproportionate into
sulfuric acid and the element. Similarly, any
moderately powerful oxidizing agent should be
capable of oxidizing the intermediate oxoacids
to sulfuric acid (Sometimes with concurrent
precipitation of sulfur) though by suitable choice
of conditions it is often possible to obtain
kinetically stable intermediate oxidation states
(eg. the polythionates with the stable S-S
linkages). It follows that all the oxoacids except
HzSO, are modetately strong reducing agents
(see below).

708 Sulfur Ch. 15

Industrial Manufacture of Sulfuric Acid

Sulfuric acid is the world's most important industrial chemical and is the cheapest bulk acid available in every country
Of the world. It was one of the first chemicals to be produced commercially in the USA (by John Harrison, Philadelphia,
1793): in Europe the history of its manufacture goes back even further — by at least two ceaturies."**"™) Concentrated
sulfuric acid (“ol of vitriol”) was frst made by the distillation of “green vitrot”, FeSOs.nH2O and was needed in quantity
to make NazSOx from NaCl for use in the Leblanc Process (p. 71). This expensive method was replaced in the early
eighteenth century by the burning of sulfur and Chile saltpeire (NaNOs) in the necks of large glass vessels containing a

le water. The process was patented in 1749 by Joshua Ward (the Quack of Hoganth’s Harlot's Progress) though it had
been in use for several decades previously in Germany. France and England. The price plumetted 20-fold from £2 to 2
shillings per pound. It dropped by a further factor of 10 by 1830 following firstly John Roebuck's replacement (ca, 1755)
Of the fragile glass jars by Iead-chambers of 200 1 (5.7 m3) capacity, and, secondly, the discovery (by N. Clement and
€, B, Désormes in 1793) that the amount of NaNOs could be substantially reduced by admitting air for the combustion
of sulfur. By 1860 James Muspratt (UK) was using lead chambers of $6000 capacity (1585 m3) and the process was,
continuous. The maximum concentration of acid that could be produced by this method was about 78% and unt 1870,
virtually the only source of oleum was the Nordhausen works (distillation of FeSO, HO). Today both processes have
‘een almost enttely replaced by the modern contact process. This derives originally from Peregrine Philips’ observation
(patented in 1831) that SO can be oxidized to SOs by air in the presence of a platinuin catalyst

“The modem process uses  potassium-sulfate-promoted. vanadium(V) oxide catalyst on a silica ot kieselguhr
support." The SO> is oblained either by burning pure sulfur ot by roasting sulfide minerals (p. 651) notably iron
Dyfite, of ores of Cu, Ni and Zn during the production of these metals. On a worldwide basis about 65% of the SO>
comes from the burning of sulfur and some 35% by the roasting of sulfide ores but in some countries (¢. the UK) over
‘95% comes from the former.

‘The oxidation of S02 to SOs is exothermic and reversible:

 

   

  

 

 

 

  

 

SO: + $0; === SOs: AH = 98 ki mol”!

According to le Chatelie's principle the vield of SO; will increase with increase in pressure, inctease in excess O>
concentration. and removal of SO3 from the reaction zone: each of these factors will also increase the rate of conversion
somewhat (by the law of mass action). Reaction rate will also increase substantially with inctease in temperature but this
will simultaneously decrease the yield of the exothermic forward reaction. Accordingly, a catalyst is required to accelerate
the reaction without diminishing the yield. Optimum conditions involve an equimolar feed of O2/SO3 (ie. ait/SO»:5/1) and
‘a estage catalytic converter operating atthe temperatures shown in the Figure." (The V;Os catalyst iy inactive below
400°C and breaks down above 620°C; it is dispersed as a thin film of molten sat on the catalyst suppor.) Such a converter
may be 13m high, 9m in diameter, contain 80 tonnes of catalyst pellets and produce 500 tonnes per day of acid. The gas
temperature rises during passage through the catalyst bed and is recooled by passage through external heat-exchanger loops
between the first three stages. In the most modern “double-absorption” plants (IPA) the SO, is removed at this stage before
the residual SO:/O> is passed through a fourth catalyst bed for final conversion. The SO3 gas cannot be absorbed directly
in water because it would first come into contact with the water-vapour above the absorbant and so produce a stable
mist of fine droplets of H2SOs which would then pass right through the absorber and out into the atmosphere. Instead.
absorption is effected by 98% HzSO4 in ceramic-packed towers and sufficient wate is added to the citculating acid to
‘maintain the required concentration. Commercial cone HzSOu is generally 96-98% to prevent undesirable sol
of the product. The main construction materials of the sulfur burner. catalytic converter. absorption towers and ducting
are mild steel and stainless stel, and the major impurity in the acid is therefore Fe" (JOppm) together with traces of SO:
and NO,.

As the molecular weight of H:SO, is 98.078 it
follows that 1 kg contains 10.196 mol: hence the
predominant ions are present to the extent of
about I millimole per mole of H>SOs and the
total concentration of species in equilibrium with
the parent acid is 4.16 millimole per mole. Many
of the physical and chemical properties of anhy:
drous H2S0, as a nonaqueous solvent stem from
these equilibria,

‘The reaction with HNOs is quantitative, and the
presence of large concentrations of the nitronium
jon, NOz*, in solutions of HNO3, MNO3 and
N20s in H;SO, enable a detailed interpretation
to be given of the nitration of aromatic hydrocar-
bons by these solutions.

Because of the high acidity of H2SOs itself,
bases form the largest class of electrolytes and
only few acids (proton donors) are known in this
solvent system. As noted above. H2S20> acts as
a proton donor to HSOs and HSO¥F is also
weak acid:

HSO,F + HySO, —= HjSO,* + SOF

One of the few strong acids is tetra(hydrogen

can be oblained by dissolving boric acid in
leur:

B(OH); + 34,520) —> 1,S0,"
+ [BOHSO, I" + #280.

Other strong acids are HeSn(HSO4), and
H,PD(HSOs),

‘Sulfuric acid forms salts (sulfates and
hydrogen sulfates) with many metals. These are
frequently very stable and, indeed, they are the
‘most important mineral compounds of several
of the more electropesitive elements. They have
been diseussed in detail under the appropriate
elements, Sulfates can be prepared by:

 

(a) dissolution of metals in aqueous HoSO,
(eg Fer,

(b) neutralization of aqueous H2SOq with
metal oxides or hydroxides (ez. MOH);

(©) decomposition of salts of volatile acids
(e.g, carbonates) with aqueous H:SOs:

Ch. 15

(@) metathesis between a soluble sulfate and a
soluble salt of the metal whose (insoluble)
sulfate is required (e.g. BaSO«);

(©) oxidation of metal sulfides or sulfites.

‘The sulfate ion is tetrahedral (S~O 149pm) and
can act as a monodentate, bidentate (chelating)
or bridging ligand. Examples are in Fig. 15.29.
Vibrational spectroscopy is a useful diagnostic,
2 the progressive reduction in local symmetry of
the SO, group from Ty to Cyp and eventually Coy
increases the number of infrared active modes
from 2 to 6 and 8 respectively. and the number of
Raman active modes from 4 10 6 and 9. (The
effects of erystal symmetry and the overlapping
cof bands complicates the analysis but correct
assignments are frequently still possible.)

the trsulfate ion S)0,q?~ is known and also
the pentasulfate ion, SsOye2” whose structure
indicates an alteration of S-O interatomic
distances and very long O-S distances to the
almost planar terminal SOs groups:

It is colourless, beautifully crystalline, and melts
at 45°, but should be handled carefully because
of the danger of explosions. It can also be made
by the action of cone HaSO, on peroxodisulfates,
and is formed as a byproduct during the
preparation of H»S20g by electrolysis of aqueous
H,S04 (N. Caro, 1898). Its salts, which are
preferably called trioxoperoxosulfates(2—) rather
than peroxomonosulfates,"™) are unstable and
the compound has few uses except those
dependent on the formation of the H2O2 during
its decomposition. The structure of the anion
{HOOSO3}", which is the active principle
of Caro’s acid, has been determined by X-
ray analysis of the hydrated salt KHSOs.H20;
selected dimensions are O-O 140.0, S—Oy

Peroxodisulfuric acid, H2SOx, is a colourless
solid mp 65° (with decomposition). The acid
is soluble in water in all proportions and its
most important salts, (NH,)2S203 and K>S2Og,
are also freely soluble. These salts are, in
fact, easier to prepare than the acid and
both are made on an industrial scale by
anodic oxidation of the corresponding sulfates
under carefully controlled conditions (high
current density, T < 30°, bright Pt electrodes,
protected cathode). The structure of the peroxo-
disulfate ion [now preferably called hexaoxo-p-
peroxodisulfate(2-)]"" is O;SOOSO;?- with
0-0 131 pm and S-O 150pm. The compounds
are used as oxidizing and bleaching agents. Thus,
as can be seen from Table 15.19, the standard
reduction potential S:Og?-/HSO,~ is 2.123 V,
and E°(SO?-/SO,7-) is similar (2.010 V);
these are more positive than for any other
aqueous couples except H2N2O2, 2H*/No, 2H20
(2.85 V), F2/2F~ (2.87 V) and F,2H* /2HF(aq)
(3.06) — see also O(g), 2H*/H20 (2.42V),
OH,H*/H,0 (2.8 V).

Thiosutfuric acid, HzS203

Attempts to prepare thiosulfurie acid by
acidification of stable thiosulfates are invariably
thwarted by the ready decomposition of the free
acid in the presence of water. The reaction
is extremely complex and depends on the
conditions used, being dominated by numerous,
redox interconversions amongst the products:
these ean include sulfur (partly as exclo-Sq), SO,
HAS, HoS,. HsSO, and various poiythionates. In
the absence of water. however. these reactions
are avoided and the parent acid is more stable:
it decomposes quantitatively below 0° according
to the reaction H»S,0; —> HyS + SOs (ef. the
analogous decomposition of H2SO; to HO and
SOs above its bp ~300"). Successful anhydrous
syntheses have been devised by M. Schmidt and
his group (1959-61), e.g.:

oon aqueous solutions of sulfites:

This reaction is the basis for the use of thiosul-
fates as “antichlorine” in the bleaching industry
where they are used to destroy any excess of
Cl, in the fibres. Bromine, being intermediate
between iodine and chlorine, can cause S,0;?~ to
act either as a I-electron or an 8-electron reducer
according to conditions. For example, in an amus-
ing and instructive experiment, if concentrated
aqueous solutions of $203? and Bra are titrated,
and the titration is then repeated after having
diluted both the S:03*~ and Br2 solutions 100-
fold, then the titre will be found to have increased
by a factor of exactly 8.

Polythionic acids, H2S,Os

“The numerous acids and salts in this group have
a venerable history and the chemistry of sys-
tems in which they occur goes back to John
Dalton's studies (1808) of the effect of H»S on
aqueous solutions of SO;. Such solutions are
now named after H.W. F. Wackenroder (1846)
‘who subjected them to systematic study. Work
during the following 60-80 y indicated the pres-
ence of numerous species including, in partic
ular, the tetrathionate S4Os- and pentathion-
ate S<Og2" ions. New perceptions have emerged
during the past few decades as a result of the
work of H. Schmidt and others in Germany:
just as H3S can react with SOs or HSO3CI to
yield thiosulfurie acid, H2S203 (p. 714), 80 reac-
tion with HyS> yields “disulfane monosulfonic
acid”, HS;SOsH; likewise polysulfanes H2Sy
(x =2-6) yield HS,SOsH. Reaction at both
ends of the polysulfane chain would yield “poly-
sulfane disulfonic acids” HOySS,SOsH which
are more commonly called potythionic acids
(HS, ,204). Many synthetic routes are available,
though mechanistic details are frequently obscure
because of the numerous simultaneous and com-
peting redox, catenation and disproportionation
reactions that occur. Typical examples include:

 

(a) Interaction of HS and SO) in Wacken-
roder's solution (see above).

(b) Reaction of chlorosulfanes with HSO3~ or
HS:0r-. eg.

Sulfurous acid, HzSOs

Sulfurous acid has never been isolated as a pure
compound, although it has recently been detected
in the gas phase by neutralization reionization
mass spectrometry (NRMS) following the facile
dissociative ionization (70eV) of either diethyl
sulfite or ethanesulfonic acid:""

Sulfites and hydrogen sulfites are moderately

‘or sulfate. The reaction with jodie is quantitative

Thiosulfates also result from reduction of SO,2~
or HSO;- with elemental sulfur (p. 714),
whereas reduction with HS. in Wacken-
roder's solution (pp. 716-7) yields polythion-
ates. It is also notable that the sulfite ion
is involved in the G-lecton sulfite reduc-
tase reaction: SO,*~ + 6H* + 66" —> SP +
3H20: E* =0.380V. Indeed, there are only
three such 6e™ reductions known in the whole
of biology, the other two being nitrite reduc-
tase (NO:~ + 7H" +60" —> NH; + 21,0)
and nitrogenase (Nz + 6H* + 6e~ —> 2NHs)

Crystallization of Na,SO3 above 37° gives
the anhydrous salt; below this temperature
‘NapSO3.7H,0 is obtained. World production of
the anhydrous salt exceeds | million tonnes pa;
most is used in the paper pulp industry, but
other applications are as an Oz scavenger in
boiler-water treatment, and as a reducing agent in
photography. Similarly, KSO3.2H0 is obtained
by passing SO, into aqueous KOH until samples
of the solution are neutral to phenolphthalein. For
a compilation of critically evaluated solubility
data, see ref. 205

Disulfurous acid, H,S20s

Like “sulfurous acid”, disulfurous acid is
unknown either in the free state or in
solution. However, as indicated in the preceding,
section, its salts, are readily obtained from
concentrated solutions of hydrogen sulfite:
2HSOs~ = $,03?- + HO. Unlike disulfates
(p. 712), diphosphates (p. 522), etc., disulfites
condense by forming an SS bond. As indicated
in Fig. 15.32a this S-S bond is rather long, but,
the S~O distances are unexceptional.

between NaHSO; and NaBH, (p. 167). Its main
use is as a reducing agent in dyeing, bleaching
of paper pulp, straw, clay, soaps, etc., and in
chemical reductions (see below). Current world-
wide demand is about 300 000 tonnes per annum.
The dithionite ion has a remarkable eclipsed
structure of approximate Cz, symmetry
(Fig. 15.32b). The extraordinarily long S-S dis-
tance (239 pm) and the almost parallel SO» planes
(dihedral angle 30°) are other unusual features.
Electron-spin-resonance studies have shown the
presence of the SO,* radical ion in solu-
tion (~300ppm), suggesting the establishment
of a monomer-dimer equilibrium S,0,?- ==
2SO;. Consistent with this, air-oxidation of alka-
line dithionite solutions at 30-60° are of order
one-half with respect to [S20,?~]. Acid hydroly-
sis (second order with respect to [S20;7~}) yields
thiosulfate and hydrogen sulfite, whereas alkaline
hydrolysis produces sulfite and sulfide:

28,0,?> + HzO —> $203?> + 2HSO,~
3NayS204 + 6NaOH —> 5NazSOs + NaxS + 3420
Hydrated dithionites can be dehydrated by gentle
warming, but the anhydrous salts themselves
decompose on further heating. For example,
NajS20, decomposes rapidly at 150° and
violently at 190°:

2NaS204 ——> NaS203 + NapSO3 + SO2

Dithionites are strong reducing agents and will
reduce dissolved O2, HO2, Ip, 10;~ and MnOg~.

Likewise Cr! is reduced to Crl" and TiO? to
Til", Heavy metal ions such as Cul, Ag!, Pbl',
Sb!" and Bi" are reduced to the metal. Many of
these reactions are useful in water-treatment and.
pollution control.

15.2.7 Sulfur-nitrogen
compounds 96-210)

The study of SN compounds is one of the
most active areas of current inorganic research:
many novel cyclic and acyclic compounds are
being prepared which have unusual structures
and which pose considerable problems in terms
of simple bonding theory. The discovery in
1975 that the polymer (SN), is a metal
whose conductivity increases with decrease in

SiNe is kinetically stable in air but is
endothermic with respect (0 its elements
(AH; 460 + 8k mol") and may detonate when
struck or when heated rapidly, This is due more

lements interchanged. The S-N distance of

‘Undoubtedly the most exciting reaction of

jin the solid stale at room temperature to

# Page 743

§15.2.7

 

Soleo

Figure 15.36 Structure of fibrous (SN), and its relation to SN2.

(©) Polythiazyl, (SN),.) Polymeric sulfur
nitride, also known as polythiazyl, was first
prepared by F. B. Burt in 1910 using a method
that is still often used today — the solid-state
polymerization of crystalline SjN2 at room,
temperature (or preferably at °C over several
days). Despite the bronze colour and metallic
lustre of the polymer, over 50 y were to elapse
before its metallic electrical conductivity, thermal
conductivity and thermoelectric effect were
investigated. By 1973 it had been established that,
(SN), was indeed a metal down to liquid helium
temperatures, and in 1975 the polymer was
shown to be a superconductor below 0.26 K. (For
higher-quality crystals the transition temperature
rises to 0.33K.) Values of the conductivity o
depend on the purity and crystallinity of the
polymer and on the direction of measurement,
being much greater along the fibres (b-axis)
than across them. At room temperature typical
values of oj are 1000-40000hm~!em-', and
this increases by as much as 1000-fold on cooling,
to 4.2K. Typical values of the anisotropy ratio
04/01, are ~50 at room temperature and ~1000
at 40K.

The mechanism of formation of $2Nz from
SsNq and of the subsequent polymeriz:
(SN)x have been much studied and are very

to deposit thin films of (SN), on a variety of
surfaces.229)

(SN); is much more stable than its precursor
S2N2. When heated in air it decomposes
explosively at about 240°C but it sublimes readily
in vacuum at about 135°. The crystal structure
reveals an almost planar chain polymer with
the dimensions shown in Fig. 15.36. The S
and N atoms deviate by about 17pm from the
mean plane. The structure should be compared

730 Sulfur

(i) Sulfur-nitrogen cations and anions

Numerous charged sulfur-nitrogen species have
been synthesized in recent years, particularly
those having an odd number of N atoms which
would otherwise be paramagnetic. However, thio
analogues of nitrites (NOz~, p. 461) and nitrates.
(NO5-, p. 465) are unknown.

The simplest stable sulfur-nitrogen species is
the cation [SN]* which was first prepared by
the direct fluoride-ion transfer reaction between
NSF and AsFs or SbFs.2%) [NS]*[AsFe]~ can
also be prepared by reaction of an excess of
AsFs with SsN3F; or by thermal decomposition
of [S3NaF2]*[AsF6]~, but the simplest high-yield
synthesis is by the reaction of SsNsCls with an
excess of AgAsF¢ in liquid SO,:7""”

AgASF, + 1/3S3N3Cls —> AgCl + [SNJ*[AsFe}~
(75% yield)

The cation has considerable synthetic potential
for a wide range of S/N compounds, e.g.2312)

802
ISN]*[AsFe]” + 1/8Sj —> [S2N]*[ASFe]~
(50% yield)

ISNI*TASFe]~ + CsF "> NSF + CsAsFo
(80% yield)
[SNJ*[SbF6]~ + [Re(CO)sBr] —>
[Re(CO);NS]}*[SbF6]” (100% yield)

Thionitrosyl complexes have already been
briefly mentioned on p. 453 and have recently
been reviewed.) They were first made?)
by reacting azido complexes directly with

The dithionitronium cation [S,N]*, which is

or CHCl, as solvent.2*”) An X-ray structure

isoelectronic with CS; and NOz+. 9) The rather
short N-S distance of 146.4 pm is consistent with
the formulation [s=N=S]*.

been obtained as a stable crystalline salt. X-

be a planar 5-membered ring with approximate

conformation of the ring is retained, but

and adjacent S-N bonds. The dictatic
is only stable in the crystalline phase; in
SO2 solutions it reversibly dissociates into the
paramagnetic species [SNJ* and [SNS]*, the
cycloaddition in the solid state apparently being
driven by the high lattice energy of the 1:2 salt.

‘These compounds contain some fascinating
and subtle structural and bonding problems.
For example, the compound [SgNq]**[SbFe]~-
[SbsFi1]~ shows two structurally distinct cations,
one with essentially equal S-N distances around
the planar ring (Fig. 15.40b) and the other, also
planar, but with alternating S-N distances of
ca. 153 and 162pm and with bond angles at

is closely related to that of S,Ng (and S,Ns*), one

The anion SsN3~ can be obtained by the
action of azides (or metallic K) on S4Ny or the
reaction of KH on S4(NH)«.°5" Further reaction
of S3N3~ with S,Ny yields S4Ns~ (as above).
The structure of S3N3~ (Fig. 15.42b) is a planar
ring of approximate Dy, symmetry.25" This has
interesting bonding implications. Thus each S
in a heterocycle forms a a bond to each of
its neighbours (thereby using 2 electrons) and it
also has an exocyclic lone-pair of electrons: this
leaves 2 electrons to contribute to the system
of the heterocycle (which might or might not
involve S 3d orbitals). Likewise, each N atom
has 2 electrons in o bonds, one exocyclic lone
pair, and contributes one electron to the x system.
Planar S-N heterocycles having 410 ring atoms
are now known and all except the radical cation
S3N2* have (4n + 2)z electrons where n = 1, 2,
or 3 as shown below:

revealed the presence of the unique acyclic anion
[SSNSS]- whose structure is in Fig. 15.42c.

S;NH is a stable pale-yellow compound, mp
113.5%; the structure is closely related to that of
cyclo-Sg as shown in Fig. 15.43a. The proton is
acidic and undergoes many reactions of which
the following are typical (see also p. 729):
BX; —> S;N-BX2+HX (X =Cl,Br)
NaCPhs —> S;NNa + PhyCH

# Page 752

736 ‘Sulfur

The 3 isomeric compounds S¢(NH)2 form
stable colourless crystals and have the structures
illustrated in Fig. 15.43b, c, and 4.226) The
1,3-, 1.4-, and 1,5-isomers melt at 130°, 133°
and 155° respectively. The 1,3,3- and 1,3,6-
triimides melt with decomposition at 128°
and 133° (Fig. 15.43e and f). The tetraimide,
Ss(NH), (mp 145°) is structurally very similar
(Fig. 15.43g):2 the N_ atoms are each
essentially trigonal planar and the heterocycle
is somewhat flattened, the distance between the
planes of the 4 N atoms and 4 S atoms being only
57pm. The influence of extensive intermolecular
H-bonding on the structure has been studied by
electron deformation density techniques.2)

Alkyl derivatives such as 1,4-Sg(NR)2_ and
Ss(NR)s can be synthesized by reacting S2Cl>
with primary amines RNH2 in an inert solvent.
‘Compounds such as 1,4-S2(NR)s (R = —CO2Et)
are now also well characterized.°) The bis-
adduct [Ag(SaN«Hg)2]* has been isolated as its
perchlorate; this has a sandwich-like structure and
is unique in being S-bonded rather than N-bonded
to the metal ion. 25)

(iv) Other cyclic sulfur-nitrogen
‘compounds ®7-298)

Incorporation of a third heteroatom into S-N
compounds is now well established, e.g. for C,
Si; P, As; O; Sn and Pb, together with the S2N2
chelates of Fe, Co, Ni, Pd and Pt mentioned on
p. 725. The field is very extensive but introduces
no new concepts into the general scheme
of covalent heterocyclic molecular chemistry
Ilustrative examples are in Fig. 15.44 and fuller

A selenium analogue of the dimer SgN.?*

characterized, viz. [SN2Se2Se2N2S}?* 5

(v) Sulfur-nitrogen halogen
compounds 67-9)

As with sulfur-halogen compounds (pp. 683-93)
the stability of N-S-X compounds decreases with
increase in atomic weight of the halogen. There are
numerous fluoro and chloro derivatives but bromo
and iodo derivatives are virtually unknown except
for the nonstoichiometric (SNX,)x._ polymers
(p. 728) and S(NX)p (p. 740). Unlike the H
atoms in the sulfur imides (p. 735) the halogen
atoms are attached to S rather than N. Fluoro
derivatives have been known since 1965 but some
of the chloro compounds have been known for
over a century. The simplest compounds are the
nonlinear thiazyl halides N=S-F and N=S-Cl
these form a noteworthy contrast to the nonlinear
nitrosyl halides O=N—X. In all cases, the pairs of
elements directly bonded are consistent with the
rule that the most electronegative atom of the trio
bonds to the least electronegative, i. {S(NH)}«,
{N(SF)}i,3.4. {N(SCDh1,3, O(NF), O(NCD (formal
Pauling electronegativities: H 2.1, S 2.5, N 3.0,
13.0, 03.5, F 4.0).

Thiazyl fluoride, NSF, is a colourless, reactive,
Pungent gas (mp —89°, bp +0.4°). It is best
prepared by the action of HgF2 on a slurry of
SaNs and CCl, but it can also be made by a
variety of other reactions:257)

 

 

cc

SiNa + 4HgF, (or AgF,) —> 4NSF + 2Hg,F)
Fs so”

SuNg —+ (SsNs(NSF)3} —> SuNz + 4NSF

'S4Nq can also be fluorinated to NSF (and other
products) using F) at 75°, SeF, at —10°, or

been determined by microwave spectroscopy:
N-S 145pm, S-F 164pm, angle at $ 116.5°.
‘The angle at S is very close to the angle at
N in ONX (110-117°, p. 442). NSF can be
stored at room temperature in copper or teflon
vessels but it slowly decomposes in glass (more
rapidly at 200°) to form a mixture of OSF2,
SO2, SiFs, S4Nq and Np. At room temperature
and at pressures above 1 atm it trimerises to
cyclo-N3SsF3 (see below) but at lower pressures
it affords S4Nq admixed with yellow-green
crystals of SyNoF2; this latter is of unknown
structure but may well be the nonlinear acyclic
species FSN=S—=NSF. N;S;F; is best made

 

by fluorinating cyclo-NySsCly with AgF/CCly.

NaS4F shows a pronounced alternation in S—N
distances and only 2 of the F atoms are axial;
it will also be noted that the conformation of
the NaS« ring is very different to that in SgNq
(p. 723) oF Sa(NH)q (Pp. 735). It is an interesting
intellectual exercise to attempt to rationalize these
striking structural differences.” The chemistry
of these various NSF oligomers has not been
extensively studied. N3S3F) is stable in dry air
but is hydrolysed by dilute aqueous NaOH to
give NH,F and sulfate. NaSsF; is reported to
form an N-bonded 1:1 adduct with BF; whereas
with AsFs or SbF5 fluoride ion transfer occurs
(accompanied by dethiazylation of the ring) to
give [N3SF2]*[MFe]~ and [NS]*{MF6~].

270. M. OWEN and A. T. BRookER, A Guide 1 Modern

In the chloro series, the compounds to be
considered are N=S-Cl, cyclo-Ny83Chy, cyclo-
NsSsClsO3, and cyclo-NSaCla; the ionic com-
pounds [S4N3}*CI~ and [cyclo-NzS,Cl]*CI- and
[catena-N(SC1)p]*[BCl4}; together with various
isomeric oxo- and fluoro-chloro derivatives. Thi-
azyl chloride, NSCI, is best obtained by pyrolysis
of the trimer in vacuum at 100°. It can also be
made by the reaction of Cl, on NSF (note that
NSF +; —> NSF) and by numerous other
reactions.) It is a yellow-green gas that rapidly
trimerizes at room temperature, and is isostruc-
tural with NSF.

By far the most common compound in the
series is N3SsCly (yellow needles, mp 168°)
which can be prepared by the direct action
of Ch (or SOCk) on SiNs in CCl, and
which is also obtained in all reactions leading
to NSCI. The structure (Fig. 15.46a) is very

---
# Page 755

§15.2.7

similar to that of NsS3F; and comprises a
slightly puckered ring with equal S-N distances
of 160.5pm and the N atoms only 18pm
above and below the plane of the 3 S atoms.
N3S3Cl; is sensitive to moisture and is oxidized
by SOs above 100° to N3SCl,05; at lower
temperatures the adduct N3SCls.6SO; is formed
and this dissociates at 100° to N3S3Cls.3SO3.
A more efficient preparation of N3S3Cl,;03
is by thermal decomposition of the product
obtained by the reaction of amidosulfuric acid
with PCls:

H)NSO3H + 2PCl; ——+> 3HC1 + OPC
+ CISO)-N=PCl;

hat

The compound is obtained in two isomeric
forms from this reaction: a, mp 145° and ,
mp 43°. The structure of the a-form is in
Fig. 15.46b and is closely related to that of
(NSCs with uniform S-N distances around
the ring. The f-form may have a different
ring conformation but more probably involves
cis-trans isomerism of the pendant Cl and O
atoms. Fluorination of a-N3S3Cl0; with KF
in CCl, yields the two isomeric fluorides cis-
N3S3F303 (mp 17.4°) and trans-N3S3F303 (mp
—12.5°) (Fig. 15.46c), The structural assignment
of the 2 isomers was made on the basis of
‘°F nmr. Fluorination with SbF; under reduced
pressure yields both the monofluoro and difluoro
derivatives N3S3CloFO3 and NjS;CIF203, each
having 3 isomers which can be separated
chromatographically and assigned by !°F nmr as
indicated schematically in Fig. 15.47. Numerous
other derivatives are known in which one or more
halogen atom is replaced by -NH>, -N=SF2,
-N=PCls, -N=CHPh, -OSiMey, etc.

The crystal structure again reveals an ionic
formulation, [N2S3CI]*CI-, this time with a
slightly puckered 5-membered ring carrying a
single pendant Cl atom as shown in Fig. 15.48;
the alternation of SN distances and the rather
small angles at the 2 directly linked S atoms are
notable features. Reaction of [NzS3Cl]*CI” with
bis(trimethylsilyl)eyanamide, (Me3Si),NCN, in

(vi) Sulfur-nitrogen-oxygen
compounds 07)

This is a classic area of inorganic chemistry
dating back to the middle of the last century
and only a brief outline will be possible. It will
be convenient first to treat the sulfur nitrogen
oxides and then the amides, imides and nitrides
of sulfuric acid. Hydrazides and hydroxylamides
of sulfuric acid will also be considered. Some
of these compounds have remarkable properties
and some are implicated in the lead-chamber
process for the manufacture of H2SOx (p. 708).
The field is closely associated with the names of
the great German chemists E. Frémy (~1845),
A. Claus (~1870), F. Raschig (~1885~1925),
W. Traube (~1890-1920), F. Ephraim (~1910),
P. Baumgarten (~1925) and, in more recent
years, M. Becke-Goehring (~1955) and F. Seel
(~1955-65).
(a) Sulfur-nitrogen oxides. Trisulfur dinitrogen
dioxide, S;N2O2, is best made by treating SzNq
boiling OSCh under a stream of SO:

S4Nz + 20SCl, —> S320; + 2Cl + S2N2 +S.

 

 

It is a yellow solid with an acyclic structure
(Fig. 15.492), cf NzOs (p. 458). Moist air
converts $3N20> to SO and SNg whereas SO

(b) Amides of sulfuric acid. Amidosulfuric acid
(better known as sulfamic acid, H{H:NSO3)), is
a classical inorganic compound and an impor-
tant industrial chemical. Formal replacement of
both hydroxyl groups in sulfuric acid leads to sul-
famide (HpN)2SO> (p. 742) which is also clearly
related structurally to the sulfuryl halides X2SO2
(p. 694).

Sulfamic acid can be made by many routes,
including addition of hydroxylamine to SO2 and
addition of NHs to SOs:

H,NOH + SO, —> HIH)NSO3]
NH; + SO; —> H[H,NSO3]

The industrial synthesis uses the strongly
exothermic reaction between urea and anhydrous
H2SO, (or dilute oleum):

(HN),CO + 2H2S0; —> CO?
+ HIH)NSO3] + NH,[HSO,]

Figure 15.50 The structures of (a) sulfamic acid, (b) the sulfamate ion, and (c) sulfamide,

Salts are obtained by direct neutralization of
the acid with appropriate oxides, hydroxides, or
carbonates. Sulfamic acid is a dry, non-volatile,
non-hygroscopic, colourless, white, crystalline
solid of considerable stability. It melts at 205°,
begins to decompose at 210°, and at 260° rapidly
gives a mixture of SO2, SOs, Nz, H20, etc. It
is a strong acid (dissociation constant 1.01 x
10-! at 25° solubility ~25g per 100g Hz0)
and, because of its physical form and stability,
is a convenient standard for acidimetry. Over
50000 tonnes are manufactured annually and its
principal applications are in formulations for
metal cleaners, scale removers, detergents and
stabilizers for chlorine in aqueous solution.27)
Its salts are used in flame retardants, weed killers
and for electroplating.

In the solid state sulfamic acid forms a strongly
H-bonded network which is best described in
terms of zwitterion units *H3NSO3~ rather than
the more obvious formulation as aminosulfuric
acid, HzNSO,(OH). The zwitterion has the
staggered configuration shown in Fig. 15.50a and
the S-N distance is notably longer than in the
sulfamate ion or sulfamide.

Dilute aqueous solutions of sulfamic acid are
stable for many months at room temperature but
at higher temperatures hydrolysis to NH[HSOg]
sets in. Alkali metal salts are stable in neutral and,

Encyclopedia ‘of Chemical Technology, 3ed edn., Vol. 21,
pp. 940-60, Wiley, New York, 1983.

alkaline solutions even at the bp. Sulfamic acid
is a monobasic acid in water (see Fig. 15.50b for
structure of the sulfamate ion). In liquid ammonia
solutions it is dibasic and, with Na for example, it
forms NaNH.SOsNa. Sulfamic acid is oxidized to
nitrogen and sulfate by Clz, Brz and C1O;, e.g.:

2H[H2NSOs] + KCIO; —> Np + 2H,SO;
+KCI+H20

Concentrated HNO, yields pure N20 whilst
aqueous HNO) reacts quantitatively to give Nz:

H[H,NSO,] + HNO; ——> H,SO, + H20+N20

hypochlorous acid by the reverse hydrolysis:

Ch + H,O = HOCI + HCL

Sulfamide, (H2N)2SO2, can be made by
ammonolysis of SO; or O2SCh.. It is a colourless

in water to give a neutral non-electrolytic solution
but in boiling water it decomposes to ammonia
and sulfuric acid. The structure (Fig. 15.50c)

Both sets of names are used in the literature.
Free imidodisulfuric acid HN(SO3H)2 (which is
isoelectronic with disulfuric acid H2S207, p. 705)
and free nitridotrisulfuric acid N(SOsH)s_ are
unstable, but their salts are well characterized and
have been extensively studied.

Imidodisulfuric acid derivatives can be pre-
pared from urea by using less sulfuric acid than
required for sulfamic acid (p. 741):

 

4(HQN)2CO + SH2S04 “> 4CO,

+ 2HN(SO,NH,)p + (NH4)2SO3

Addition of aqueous KOH liberates NH3 and
affords crystalline HN(SO3K) on evaporation.
All 3 H atoms in HN(SO}H)2 can be replaced
by NH, or M!, e.g. the direct reaction of NH;
and SO, yields the triammonium salt:

4NHy + 2SO; —> NH,[N(SONH4)2]

potassium salts. Comparison with the hydroxyl-
amine derivative K[HN(OH)SO3] (below) is also

Fluoro and chloro derivatives of imidodisul-
furic acid can be made by reacting HSO3F or

(HpN)2CO + 3HSO3F —+ CO, + HN(SO,F)
+ [NHa][HSOs] + HF

HN(SO;F)2 melts at 17°, boils at 170° and can
be further fluorinated with elemental F, at room
temperature to give FN(SO2F)2, mp —79.9°,
bp 60°. The chloro derivative HN(SO,CI)2 is a
white crystalline compound, mp 37°: it is made in

reaction sequence:

2PCls + HoNHSO; ——> ClhP=NSO;CI
+ OPCh + HCI
ChP=NSO,Cl + CISO3H —>
HN(SO,Cl)2 + OPCh;

KNO; + 4KHSO; —> N(SO;K);
+ K2SO; + 2H20

16

Selenium, Tellurium

and Polonium

16.1 The Elements"-®

16.1.1 Introduction: history,
abundance, distribution

Tellurium was the first of these three elements to
be discovered. It was isolated by the Austrian
chemist F. J. Miller von Reichenstein in 1782
a few years after the discovery of oxygen
by J. Priestley and C. W. Scheele (p. 600),
though the periodic group relationship between
the elements was not apparent until nearly
a century later (p. 20). Tellurium was first

'K.W. BaGNAaLL, Selenium, tellurium and polonium,
Chap. 24 in Comprehensive Inorganic Chemistry, Vol. 2,
pp. 935-1008, Pergamon Press, Oxford, 1973.

2R_ A. Zincano and W. C. CooreR (eds.), Selenium, Van
Nostrand, Reinhold, New York, 1974, 835 pp.

3 W.C. Corer (ed.), Tellurium, Van Nostrand, Reinhold,
New York, 1971, 437 pp.

4N, B. Mixeev, Polonium, Chemiker Zeitung 102, 277-86
(1978). ‘See also _K.W. BAGNALL, Radiochim. Acta. 32,
153~61 (1983). Polonium, Gmelin Handbook of Inorganic
and Organometallic Chemistry, Suppl. Vol. 1, Springer-
Verlag, Berlin, 1990, 425 pp.

787

observed in ores mined in the gold districts
of Transylvania; Miiller called it metallum
problematicum ot aurum paradoxum because it
showed none of the properties of the expected
antimony.) The name tellurium (Latin tellus,
earth) is due to another Austrian chemist,
M. H. Klaproth, the discoverer of zirconium and
uranium,

Selenium was isolated some 35 y after
tellurium and, since the new element resembled
tellurium, it was named from the Greek oeiivn,
selene, the moon. The discovery was made in
1817 by the Swedish chemist J. J. Berzelius
(discoverer of Si, Ce and Th) and J. G. Gahn
(discoverer of Mn);‘) they observed a reddish-
brown deposit during the burning of sulfur
obtained from Fahlun copper pyrites, and showed
it to be volatile and readily reducible to the new
element.

The discovery of polonium by Marie Curie
in 1898 is a story that has been told many

  

SM. E. Weexs, Discovery of the Elements, 6th edn., Journal
of Chemical Education, Easton, Pa., 1956: pp. 303-37.

---
# Page 764

748 Selenium, Tellurium and Polonium

times. The immense feat of processing huge
quantities of uranium ore and of following the
progress of separation by the newly discovered
phenomenon of radioactivity (together with her
parallel isolation of radium by similar techniques,
p. 108), earned her the Nobel Prize for Chemistry
in 1911. She had already shared the 1902 Nobel
Prize for Physics with H. A. Becquerel and her
husband P. Curie for their joint researches on
radioactivity. Indeed, this was the first time,
though by no means the last, that invisible
quantities of a new element had been identified,
separated, and investigated solely by means of its
radioactivity. The element was named after Marie
Curie’s home country, Poland.

Selenium and tellurium are comparatively
rare elements, being sixty-sixth and seventy-
third respectively in order of crustal abundance;
polonium, on account of its radioactive decay,
is exceedingly unabundant. Selenium comprises
some 0.05 ppm of the earth's crust and is
therefore similar to Ag and Hg, which are each
about 0.08 ppm, and Pd (0.015 ppm). Tellurium,
at about 0.002 ppm can be compared with Au
(0.004 ppm) and Ir (0.001 ppm). Both elements
are occasionally found native, in association
with sulfur, and many of their minerals occur
together with the sulfides of chalcophilic metals
(p. 648),2) e.g. Cu, Ag, Au; Zn, Cd, Hg; Fe,
Co, Ni; Pb, As, Bi. Sometimes the minerals
are partly oxidized, e.g. MSeO3.2H,0 (M = Ni,
Cu, Pb); PbTeOs, Fe2(TeOs)3.2H20, FeTeO,,
HezTeOs, BizTeO,(OH),, etc. Selenolite, SeO2,
and tellurite, TeO2, have also been found.

Polonium has no stable isotopes, all 27
isotopes being radioactive; of these only 7!°Po
occurs naturally, as the penultimate member of
the radium decay series:

6 - a
210pp ——> 2108; ——> 2!0py —“—> 26pp
a aay 8 Sora i38aga ©

RaD RaE RaF RaG

Ref. 5, Chap. 29, pp. 803-43. See also E. FaRBER,
Nobel Prize Winners in Chemistry 1901~1961, Abelard-
‘Schuman, London, Marie Sklodowska Curie, pp. 45-8.
F.C. Wooo, Marie Curie, in E. FARBER (ed.), Great
Chemists, pp. 1263-75, Interscience, New York, 1961.

Ch. 16

Because of the fugitive nature of 2!°Po, uranium
ores contain only about 0.1 mg Po per tonne of
ore (i.e. 10~* ppm). The overall abundance of Po
in crustal rocks of the earth is thus of the order
of 3 x 107" ppm.

16.1.2 Production and uses of the
elements 2-4”)

The main source of Se and Te is the anode
slime deposited during the electrolytic refining of
Cu (p. 1175); this mud also contains commercial
quantities of Ag, Au and the platinum metals.
Direct recovery from minerals is not usually
economically viable because of their rarity.
Selenium is also recovered from the sludge
accumulating in sulfuric acid plants and from
electrostatic precipitator dust collected during the
processing of Cu and Pb. Detailed procedures for
isolation and purification depend on the relative
concentrations of Se, Te and other impurities, but
atypical sequence involves oxidation by roasting
in air with soda ash followed by leaching:

 

650"
Ag)Se + Na,CO; + O02 —>

2Ag +NaSeO3 + CO?

CurSe + NazCOs +202 —>
2CuO + NazSeO3 +CO>

CurTe + NazCOs +202 —>
2CuO +.NazTeOs + COz

In the absence of soda ash, SeO2 can be
volatilized directly from the roast:

30° 650"
CupSe + $02 —+ CuO + CuSeO3 —>
2Cu0 + SeO2

700"
Ag,SeO; —> 2Ag +SeO2 + 502

  

7 Kirk-Othmer Encyclopedia of Chemical Technology, 4th
edn., 1997, Selenium and Selenium Compounds, Vol. 21,
pp. 686-719, Tellurium and tellurium compounds, Vol. 22,
Pp. 659-79, 1983,

---
# Page 765

§16.1.2

Separation of Se and Te can also be achieved
by neutralizing the alkaline selenite and tellurite
each with HSO,; this precipitates the tellurium
as a hydrous dioxide and leaves the more acidic
selenous acid, H2SeO3, in solution from which
99.5% pure Se can be precipitated by SO):*

H)SeO3 + 2SO, + H>O —> Se + 2H,SO,

Tellurium is obtained by dissolving the dioxide
in aqueous NaOH followed by electrolytic
reduction:

NazTeO3 + H»O —> Te + 2NaOH + O>

The NaOH is regenerated and only make-up
quantities are required. However, the detailed
processes adopted industrially to produce Se and
Te are much more complex and sophisticated than
this outline implies.(24”

World production of refined Se in 1995 was
~2000 tonnes the largest producers being Japan
(600 t), USA (360 t) and Canada (300 1). The
pattern of use no doubt varies somewhat from
country to country, but in the USA the largest sin-
gle use of the element (35%) is as a decolorizor
of glass (0.01-0.15 kg/tonne). Higher concentra-
tions (1~2kg/tonne) yield delicate pink glasses.
The glorious selenium ruby glasses, which are
the most brilliant reds known to glass-makers, are
obtained by incorporating solid particles of cad-
‘mium sulfoselenide in the glass; the deepest ruby
colour is obtained when Cd(S,Se) has about 10%
CAS, but as the relative concentration of CdS
increases the colour moderates to red (40% CdS),
orange (75%) and yellow (100%). Cadmium sul-
foselenides are also widely used as heat-resistant
red pigments in plastics, paints, inks and enamels.
Another very important application of elemental
Se is in xerography, which has developed dur-
ing the past four decades into the pre-eminent
process for document copying, as witnessed by

* Very pure Se can be obtained by heating the crude material
in Hy at 650° and then decomposing the HzSe so formed by
passing the gas through a silica tube at 1000". Any HS
present, being more stable than HzSe, passes through the
tube unchanged, whereas hydrides which are less stable than
H4Se, such as those of Te, P, AS, Sb, are not formed in the
inial reaction at 650°,

Production and uses of the elements 749

the ubiquitous presence of xerox machines in
offices and libraries (see Panel). Related uses
are as a photoconductor (selenium photoelec~
tric cells) and as a rectifier in semiconductor
devices (p. 258). Small amounts of ferroselenium
are used to improve the casting, forging and
machinability of stainless steels, and the dithio-
carbamate (Se(S)CNEt)s] finds some use in the
processing of natural and synthetic rubbers. Sele~
nium pharmaceuticals comprise a further small
outlet. In addition to Se, Fe/Se, Cd(S,Se) and
{Se(S:CNEt)4] the main commercially available
compounds of Se are SeO>, Na;SeO3, Na,SeOs,
HzSeO, and SeOCh, (q.v.)

Production of Te is on a much smaller
scale: ca. 350 tonnes in 1978, dominated by
USA, Canada and Japan, More than 70% of
the Te is used in iron and steel production
and in non-ferrous metals and alloys, and 25%
for chemicals. A small amount of TeO> is
used in tinting glass, and Te compounds find
some use as catalysts and as curing agents in
the rubber industry. In addition to Te, Fe/Te
and TeO3, commercially important compounds
include NayTeO, and (Te(SpCNEt.)s].

Polonium, because of its very low abundance
and very short half-life, is not obtained from
natural sources. Virtually all our knowledge of
the physical and chemical properties of the
element come from studies on ?!Po which is best
made by neutron irradiation of ™Bi in a nuclear
reactor:

1138.38 d

It will be recalled that Bi is 100% abundant
and is the heaviest stable nuclide of any element
(p. 550), but it is essential to use very high
Purity Bi to prevent unwanted nuclear side~
reactions which would contaminate the product
219Po; in particular Sc, Ag, As, Sb and Te must
be <0.1 ppm and Fe <10ppm. Polonium can
be obtained directly in milligram amounts by
fractional vacuum distillation from the metallic
bismuth. Alternatively, it can be deposited
spontaneously by electrochemical replacement
onto the surface of a less electropositive metal

750 Selenium, Tellurium and Polonlum Ch. 16

Xerography

‘The invention of xerography by C. F. Carlson (USA) in the period 1934-42 was the culmination of a prolonged and
‘concerted attack on the problem of devising a rapid. cheap and dry provess for dircet document copying, without the
need for the intermediate formation of a permanent photograph oF even the use of specially prepared
photographic paper forthe “print”. The discovery that vacuumn-deposted amorphous or vitreous selenium was the almost
‘deal photoconductor for xerography was made in the Battelle Memerial Institute (Ohio, USA) in 1948. The dramatic
success of these twin developments is witnessed by the vast nuinber of xerox machines in daily use throughout the world
today. However. early xerox equipment was not automatic. Models introduced in 1951 became popular for making offset
masters, and rotary serographic machines were introduced in 1959, but it was only after the introduction of the Xerox
914 copier in the carly 1960s that electrophotography caine of age. The word “xerography” derives from the Greek Enp6,
vero dry. Youbri, graphy. writing

“The sequential sleps involved in commercial machines which employ re
copies are shown in the Figure™ and further elucidated below.

 

    

   

 

 

 

 

ke photoreceptors for generating xerox

 

1. Sensitization of the photoreceptor, The photoreceptor consists of a vacuum-deposited film of amorphous Se, ~50 1m.
thick, on an Al substrate: this is sensitized by electrostatic charging from x corona discharge using a field of ~108 Vem~!
Exposure and laient image formation. The sensitized photoreceptor is exposed to a light and dark image pattern; in
the light areas the surface potential of the photoconductor is reduced due to a photoconductive discharge. Since current
‘can only flow perpendicular to the surface, this step produces an electrostaic-potential distribution which replicates the
patter of the image.
Development of the image. This is done using a mixture of black (or coloured) toner particles, typically 101m in
diameter, and spherical carrier beads (~100)+m diameter). The toner particles become charged triboelectrically (ie. by
tion) and are preferentially attracted either by the surface fringe field at light-dark boundaries or (in sy ha
developing electrode) by the absolute potential im the dark areas: they adhere to the photoreceptor. thus forming a visible
image corresponding 10 the latent electrostatic image.

4. Image transfer. This is best done electrostatically by charging the print paper to attract the toner panicles.

'. Print fixing. The powder image is made pennanent by fusing or melting the toner particles into the surface of the
paper, either by heat. by heat and pressure, or by solvent vapours.

6. Cleaning. Any toner still left on the photoreceptor after the transfer process is removed with a cloth web or brush,
‘or by a combination of electrostatic and mechanical means.

7. Image erasure. The potential differences due to latent image formation ate removed by flooding the photoreceptors
\with a sufficiently intense light source 10 drive the surface potential to some uniformly low value ((ypically ~100V
corresponding 10 fields of ~10! Veni"); the photoreceptor is then ready for smother print cycle.

‘The eleyance, cheapness and convenience of xerography for document copying has led o rapid commercial development
‘on a colossal scale throughout the world.

except on the trace scale (submicrogram it is an almost pure @-emitter (Eq 5.30MeV)

caused by the intense radioactivity (p. 753). All rays (Emax 0.803MeV). Because of its short
